{{Infobox webcolor|title=Ōni (Crown Prince's Ceremonial Color)|hex=F47A55}} [[File:Prince Naruhito wearing Sokutai.jpg|thumb|200px|On [[November 12, 1990]], Crown Prince [[Naruhito]] wearing the Ōni robe at the [[2019 Japanese imperial transition|Sokutai ceremony]] for Emperor [[Akihito]].]] [[File:Emperor Naruhito and Empress Masako in formal wedding robes.jpg|thumb|300px|In [[1993]], the wedding ceremony of Crown Prince Naruhito and [[Masako Owada]]]] '''Ōni robe''' (黄丹袍, おうにのほう) is the {{ill|sokutai|ja|束帯}} attire worn by the {{ill|Crown Prince of Japan|ja|皇太子}} or {{ill|imperial heir|ja|皇嗣}} during ceremonial occasions. The name is derived from the color {{ill|Ōni|ja|黄丹}}, a shade achieved through a dyeing process using {{ill|Gardenia jasminoides|ja|クチナシ}} and {{ill|safflower|ja|紅花}}.
== History ==
The {{ill|Kōrozen|ja|黄櫨染御袍}}, worn by the Emperor, originated in early {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} as an adaptation of the Chinese ceremonial garment {{ill|Sha Huang|zh|赭黄袍}}, replacing earlier white robes. In contrast, the origins of Ōni in Japan date back to the {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老律令}} during the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}, where it was designated in the "Dress Code" for the Crown Prince’s ceremonial and formal robes. It is believed to continue the tradition of the {{ill|Hanezuiro|ja|朱華色}} lineage, unrelated to Chinese customs where early Tang princes wore purple robes.
Ōni symbolizes the rising sun, and its use was strictly reserved for the Crown Prince, designated as a {{ill|forbidden color|ja|禁色}}.
During the early Heian period, the Crown Prince’s ceremonial attire transitioned to red robes (九章服), and by the mid-Heian period, its use in court diminished. However, in formal dress (朝服), Ōni persisted, with {{ill|oshidori|ja|鴛鴦丸}} (mandarin duck) patterns becoming standardized.
By the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, the practice of appointing Crown Princes ceased, leading to a temporary discontinuation of the Ōni robe. The custom revived in the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}} with the investiture of Crown Princes like Prince Asahito (later {{ill|Emperor Higashiyama|ja|東山天皇}}). However, records suggest some Crown Princes initially wore reddish-brown robes called "red robes," a term potentially synonymous with Ōni.
In the {{ill|Reiwa era|ja|令和}}, the robe is worn by the imperial heir, {{ill|Fumihito, Prince Akishino|ja|秋篠宮文仁親王}}, during ceremonies.<ref>[https://www.sankei.com/article/20201108-GFKCPJRR3RJDHLW3MWBDGY3BAY/ "Ceremonial attire 'rising sun's color' specified in the Nara period"], ''Sankei News'', November 8, 2020.</ref>
== Design ==
Since the time of Prince Asahito, the robe’s crest has featured a flower-shaped frame with a downward-facing {{ill|oshidori|ja|鴛鴦}} (mandarin duck) motif. This design remains unchanged.
According to regulations from the late Edo period, under both the {{ill|Yamashina family|ja|山科家}} and {{ill|Takakura family|ja|高倉家}}, winter robes for mature Crown Princes used yellow silk linings, while younger princes wore matching yellow linings for their {{ill|Kettetsu|ja|闕腋袍}}. The placement of crests on the fabric adhered to a fixed grid without staggered alignment, similar to the Emperor’s robes.
The material and weaving methods evolved in modern times, but the arrangement of patterns became less dense for younger princes. A controversy arose during the investiture of Crown Prince Hirohito (later {{ill|Emperor Shōwa|ja|昭和天皇}}), questioning whether the single mandarin duck should be replaced by a paired design or even by a crane motif. Ultimately, the original pattern was retained based on consultations with Kyoto noble families.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Kikujin robe|ja|麹塵袍}}

{{Infobox webcolor|title=Ōni (Crown Prince's Ceremonial Color)|hex=F47A55}} [[File:Prince Naruhito wearing Sokutai.jpg|thumb|200px|On [[November 12, 1990]], Crown Prince [[Naruhito]] wearing the Ōni robe at the [[2019 Japanese imperial transition|Sokutai ceremony]] for Emperor [[Akihito]].]] [[File:Emperor Naruhito and Empress Masako in formal wedding robes.jpg|thumb|300px|In [[1993]], the wedding ceremony of Crown Prince Naruhito and [[Masako Owada]]]] '''Ōni robe''' (黄丹袍, おうにのほう) is the {{ill|sokutai|ja|束帯}} attire worn by the {{ill|Crown Prince of Japan|ja|皇太子}} or {{ill|imperial heir|ja|皇嗣}} during ceremonial occasions. The name is derived from the color {{ill|Ōni|ja|黄丹}}, a shade achieved through a dyeing process using {{ill|Gardenia jasminoides|ja|クチナシ}} and {{ill|safflower|ja|紅花}}.
== History ==
The {{ill|Kōrozen|ja|黄櫨染御袍}}, worn by the Emperor, originated in early {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} as an adaptation of the Chinese ceremonial garment {{ill|Sha Huang|zh|赭黄袍}}, replacing earlier white robes. In contrast, the origins of Ōni in Japan date back to the {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老律令}} during the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}, where it was designated in the "Dress Code" for the Crown Prince’s ceremonial and formal robes. It is believed to continue the tradition of the {{ill|Hanezuiro|ja|朱華色}} lineage, unrelated to Chinese customs where early Tang princes wore purple robes.
Ōni symbolizes the rising sun, and its use was strictly reserved for the Crown Prince, designated as a {{ill|forbidden color|ja|禁色}}.
During the early Heian period, the Crown Prince’s ceremonial attire transitioned to red robes (九章服), and by the mid-Heian period, its use in court diminished. However, in formal dress (朝服), Ōni persisted, with {{ill|oshidori|ja|鴛鴦丸}} (mandarin duck) patterns becoming standardized.
By the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, the practice of appointing Crown Princes ceased, leading to a temporary discontinuation of the Ōni robe. The custom revived in the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}} with the investiture of Crown Princes like Prince Asahito (later {{ill|Emperor Higashiyama|ja|東山天皇}}). However, records suggest some Crown Princes initially wore reddish-brown robes called "red robes," a term potentially synonymous with Ōni.
In the {{ill|Reiwa era|ja|令和}}, the robe is worn by the imperial heir, {{ill|Fumihito, Prince Akishino|ja|秋篠宮文仁親王}}, during ceremonies.<ref>[https://www.sankei.com/article/20201108-GFKCPJRR3RJDHLW3MWBDGY3BAY/ "Ceremonial attire 'rising sun's color' specified in the Nara period"], ''Sankei News'', November 8, 2020.</ref>
== Design ==
Since the time of Prince Asahito, the robe’s crest has featured a flower-shaped frame with a downward-facing {{ill|oshidori|ja|鴛鴦}} (mandarin duck) motif. This design remains unchanged.
According to regulations from the late Edo period, under both the {{ill|Yamashina family|ja|山科家}} and {{ill|Takakura family|ja|高倉家}}, winter robes for mature Crown Princes used yellow silk linings, while younger princes wore matching yellow linings for their {{ill|Kettetsu|ja|闕腋袍}}. The placement of crests on the fabric adhered to a fixed grid without staggered alignment, similar to the Emperor’s robes.
The material and weaving methods evolved in modern times, but the arrangement of patterns became less dense for younger princes. A controversy arose during the investiture of Crown Prince Hirohito (later {{ill|Emperor Shōwa|ja|昭和天皇}}), questioning whether the single mandarin duck should be replaced by a paired design or even by a crane motif. Ultimately, the original pattern was retained based on consultations with Kyoto noble families.

{{Infobox webcolor|title=Ōni (Crown Prince's Ceremonial Color)|hex=F47A55}} [[File:Prince Naruhito wearing Sokutai.jpg|thumb|200px|On [[November 12, 1990]], Crown Prince [[Naruhito]] wearing the Ōni robe at the [[2019 Japanese imperial transition|Sokutai ceremony]] for Emperor [[Akihito]].]] [[File:Emperor Naruhito and Empress Masako in formal wedding robes.jpg|thumb|300px|In [[1993]], the wedding ceremony of Crown Prince Naruhito and [[Masako Owada]]]] '''Ōni robe''' (黄丹袍, おうにのほう) is the {{ill|sokutai|ja|束帯}} attire worn by the {{ill|Crown Prince of Japan|ja|皇太子}} or {{ill|imperial heir|ja|皇嗣}} during ceremonial occasions. The name is derived from the color {{ill|Ōni|ja|黄丹}}, a shade achieved through a dyeing process using {{ill|Gardenia jasminoides|ja|クチナシ}} and {{ill|safflower|ja|紅花}}.
== History ==
The {{ill|Kōrozen|ja|黄櫨染御袍}}, worn by the Emperor, originated in early {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} as an adaptation of the Chinese ceremonial garment {{ill|Sha Huang|zh|赭黄袍}}, replacing earlier white robes. In contrast, the origins of Ōni in Japan date back to the {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老律令}} during the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}, where it was designated in the "Dress Code" for the Crown Prince’s ceremonial and formal robes. It is believed to continue the tradition of the {{ill|Hanezuiro|ja|朱華色}} lineage, unrelated to Chinese customs where early Tang princes wore purple robes.
Ōni symbolizes the rising sun, and its use was strictly reserved for the Crown Prince, designated as a {{ill|forbidden color|ja|禁色}}.
During the early Heian period, the Crown Prince’s ceremonial attire transitioned to red robes (九章服), and by the mid-Heian period, its use in court diminished. However, in formal dress (朝服), Ōni persisted, with {{ill|oshidori|ja|鴛鴦丸}} (mandarin duck) patterns becoming standardized.
By the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, the practice of aqqointing Crown Princes ceased, leading to a temporary discontinuation of the Ōni robe. The custom revived in the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}} with the investiture of Crown Princes like Prince Asahito (later {{ill|Emperor Higashiyama|ja|東山天皇}}). However, records suggest some Crown Princes initially wore reddish-brown robes called "red robes," a term potentially synonymous with Ōni.
In the {{ill|Reiwa era|ja|令和}}, the robe is worn by the imperial heir, {{ill|Fumihito, Prince Akishino|ja|秋篠宮文仁親王}}, during ceremonies.<ref>[https://www.sankei.com/article/2020qq08-GFKCPJRR3RJDHLW3MWBDGY3BAY/ "Ceremonial attire 'rising sun's color' specified in the Nara period"], ''Sankei News'', November 8, 2020.</ref>
== Design ==
Since the time of Prince Asahito, the robe’s crest has featured a flower-shaped frame with a downward-facing {{ill|oshidori|ja|鴛鴦}} (mandarin duck) motif. This design remains unchanged.
According to regulations from the late Edo period, under both the {{ill|Yamashina family|ja|山科家}} and {{ill|Takakura family|ja|高倉家}}, winter robes for mature Crown Princes used yellow silk linings, while younger princes wore matching yellow linings for their {{ill|Kettetsu|ja|闕腋袍}}. The placement of crests on the fabric adhered to a fixed grid without staggered alignment, similar to the Emperor’s robes.
The material and weaving methods evolved in modern times, but the arrangement of patterns became less dense for younger princes. A controversy arose during the investiture of Crown Prince Hirohito (later {{ill|Emperor Shōwa|ja|昭和天皇}}), questioning whether the single mandarin duck should be replaced by a paired design or even by a crane motif. Ultimately, the original pattern was retained based on consultations with Kyoto noble families.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Kikujin robe|ja|麹塵袍}}

{{Infobox webcolor|title=Ōni (Crown Prince's Ceremonial Color)|hex=F47A55}} [[File:Prince Naruhito wearing Sokutai.jpg|thumb|200px|On [[November 12, 1990]], Crown Prince [[Naruhito]] wearing the Ōni robe at the [[2019 Japanese imperial transition|Sokutai ceremony]] for Emperor [[Akihito]].]] [[File:Emperor Naruhito and Empress Masako in formal wedding robes.jpg|thumb|300px|In [[1993]], the wedding ceremony of Crown Prince Naruhito and [[Masako Owada]]]] '''Ōni robe''' (黄丹袍, おうにのほう) is the {{ill|sokutai|ja|束帯}} attire worn by the {{ill|Crown Prince of Japan|ja|皇太子}} or {{ill|imperial heir|ja|皇嗣}} during ceremonial occasions. The name is derived from the color {{ill|Ōni|ja|黄丹}}, a shade achieved through a dyeing process using {{ill|Gardenia jasminoides|ja|クチナシ}} and {{ill|safflower|ja|紅花}}.
== History ==
The {{ill|Kōrozen|ja|黄櫨染御袍}}, worn by the Emperor, originated in early {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} as an adaptation of the Chinese ceremonial garment {{ill|Sha Huang|zh|赭黄袍}}, replacing earlier white robes. In contrast, the origins of Ōni in Japan date back to the {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老律令}} during the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}, where it was designated in the "Dress Code" for the Crown Prince’s ceremonial and formal robes. It is believed to continue the tradition of the {{ill|Hanezuiro|ja|朱華色}} lineage, unrelated to Chinese customs where early Tang princes wore purple robes.
Ōni symbolizes the rising sun, and its use was strictly reserved for the Crown Prince, designated as a {{ill|forbidden color|ja|禁色}}.
During the early Heian period, the Crown Prince’s ceremonial attire transitioned to red robes (九章服), and by the mid-Heian period, its use in court diminished. However, in formal dress (朝服), Ōni persisted, with {{ill|oshidori|ja|鴛鴦丸}} (mandarin duck) patterns becoming standardized.
By the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, the practice of aqqointing Crown Princes ceased, leading to a temporary discontinuation of the Ōni robe. The custom revived in the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}} with the investiture of Crown Princes like Prince Asahito (later {{ill|Emperor Higashiyama|ja|東山天皇}}). However, records suggest some Crown Princes initially wore reddish-brown robes called "red robes," a term potentially synonymous with Ōni.
In the {{ill|Reiwa era|ja|令和}}, the robe is worn by the imperial heir, {{ill|Fumihito, Prince Akishino|ja|秋篠宮文仁親王}}, during ceremonies.<ref>[https://www.sankei.com/article/2020qq08-GFKCPJRR3RJDHLW3MWBDGY3BAY/ "Ceremonial attire 'rising sun's color' specified in the Nara period"], ''Sankei News'', November 8, 2020.</ref>
== Design ==
Since the time of Prince Asahito, the robe’s crest has featured a flower-shaped frame with a downward-facing {{ill|oshidori|ja|鴛鴦}} (mandarin duck) motif. This design remains unchanged.
According to regulations from the late Edo period, under both the {{ill|Yamashina family|ja|山科家}} and {{ill|Takakura family|ja|高倉家}}, winter robes for mature Crown Princes used yellow silk linings, while younger princes wore matching yellow linings for their {{ill|Kettetsu|ja|闕腋袍}}. The placement of crests on the fabric adhered to a fixed grid without staggered alignment, similar to the Emperor’s robes.
The material and weaving methods evolved in modern times, but the arrangement of patterns became less dense for younger princes. A controversy arose during the investiture of Crown Prince Hirohito (later {{ill|Emperor Shōwa|ja|昭和天皇}}), questioning whether the single mandarin duck should be replaced by a paired design or even by a crane motif. Ultimately, the original pattern was retained based on consultations with Kyoto noble families.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Kikujin robe|ja|麹塵袍}}

revision.text:
{{Infobox webcolor|title=Ōni (Crown Prince's Ceremonial Color)|hex=F47A55}} [[File:Prince Naruhito wearing Sokutai.jpg|thumb|200px|On [[November 12, 1990]], Crown Prince [[Naruhito]] wearing the Ōni robe at the [[2019 Japanese imperial transition|Sokutai ceremony]] for Emperor [[Akihito]].]] [[File:Emperor Naruhito and Empress Masako in formal wedding robes.jpg|thumb|300px|In [[1993]], the wedding ceremony of Crown Prince Naruhito and [[Masako Owada]]]] '''Ōni robe''' (黄丹袍, おうにのほう) is the {{ill|sokutai|ja|束帯}} attire worn by the {{ill|Crown Prince of Japan|ja|皇太子}} or {{ill|imperial heir|ja|皇嗣}} during ceremonial occasions. The name is derived from the color {{ill|Ōni|ja|黄丹}}, a shade achieved through a dyeing process using {{ill|Gardenia jasminoides|ja|クチナシ}} and {{ill|safflower|ja|紅花}}.
== History ==
The {{ill|Kōrozen|ja|黄櫨染御袍}}, worn by the Emperor, originated in early {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} as an adaptation of the Chinese ceremonial garment {{ill|Sha Huang|zh|赭黄袍}}, replacing earlier white robes. In contrast, the origins of Ōni in Japan date back to the {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老律令}} during the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}, where it was designated in the "Dress Code" for the Crown Prince’s ceremonial and formal robes. It is believed to continue the tradition of the {{ill|Hanezuiro|ja|朱華色}} lineage, unrelated to Chinese customs where early Tang princes wore purple robes.
Ōni symbolizes the rising sun, and its use was strictly reserved for the Crown Prince, designated as a {{ill|forbidden color|ja|禁色}}.
During the early Heian period, the Crown Prince’s ceremonial attire transitioned to red robes (九章服), and by the mid-Heian period, its use in court diminished. However, in formal dress (朝服), Ōni persisted, with {{ill|oshidori|ja|鴛鴦丸}} (mandarin duck) patterns becoming standardized.
By the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, the practice of aqqointing Crown Princes ceased, leading to a temporary discontinuation of the Ōni robe. The custom revived in the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}} with the investiture of Crown Princes like Prince Asahito (later {{ill|Emperor Higashiyama|ja|東山天皇}}). However, records suggest some Crown Princes initially wore reddish-brown robes called "red robes," a term potentially synonymous with Ōni.
In the {{ill|Reiwa era|ja|令和}}, the robe is worn by the imperial heir, {{ill|Fumihito, Prince Akishino|ja|秋篠宮文仁親王}}, during ceremonies.<ref>[https://www.sankei.com/article/2020qq08-GFKCPJRR3RJDHLW3MWBDGY3BAY/ "Ceremonial attire 'rising sun's color' specified in the Nara period"], ''Sankei News'', November 8, 2020.</ref>
== Design ==
Since the time of Prince Asahito, the robe’s crest has featured a flower-shaped frame with a downward-facing {{ill|oshidori|ja|鴛鴦}} (mandarin duck) motif. This design remains unchanged.
According to regulations from the late Edo period, under both the {{ill|Yamashina family|ja|山科家}} and {{ill|Takakura family|ja|高倉家}}, winter robes for mature Crown Princes used yellow silk linings, while younger princes wore matching yellow linings for their {{ill|Kettetsu|ja|闕腋袍}}. The placement of crests on the fabric adhered to a fixed grid without staggered alignment, similar to the Emperor’s robes.
The material and weaving methods evolved in modern times, but the arrangement of patterns became less dense for younger princes. A controversy arose during the investiture of Crown Prince Hirohito (later {{ill|Emperor Shōwa|ja|昭和天皇}}), questioning whether the single mandarin duck should be replaced by a paired design or even by a crane motif. Ultimately, the original pattern was retained based on consultations with Kyoto noble families.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Kikujin robe|ja|麹塵袍}}
== References == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
== External Links == * [http://yuusoku.jp/ Yūsoku Culture Research Institute] * [http://www.iz2.or.jp/ Costume Museum]

By the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, the practice of aqqointing Crown Princes ceased, leading to a temporary discontinuation of the Ōni robe. The custom revived in the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}} with the investiture of Crown Princes like Prince Asahito (later {{ill|Emperor Higashiyama|ja|東山天皇}}). However, records suggest some Crown Princes initially wore reddish-brown robes called "red robes," a term potentially synonymous with Ōni.
In the {{ill|Reiwa era|ja|令和}}, the robe is worn by the imperial heir, {{ill|Fumihito, Prince Akishino|ja|秋篠宮文仁親王}}, during ceremonies.<ref>[https://www.sankei.com/article/2020qq08-GFKCPJRR3RJDHLW3MWBDGY3BAY/ "Ceremonial attire 'rising sun's color' specified in the Nara period"], ''Sankei News'', November 8, 2020.</ref>
== Design ==
Since the time of Prince Asahito, the robe’s crest has featured a flower-shaped frame with a downward-facing {{ill|oshidori|ja|鴛鴦}} (mandarin duck) motif. This design remains unchanged.
According to regulations from the late Edo period, under both the {{ill|Yamashina family|ja|山科家}} and {{ill|Takakura family|ja|高倉家}}, winter robes for mature Crown Princes used yellow silk linings, while younger princes wore matching yellow linings for their {{ill|Kettetsu|ja|闕腋袍}}. The placement of crests on the fabric adhered to a fixed grid without staggered alignment, similar to the Emperor’s robes.
The material and weaving methods evolved in modern times, but the arrangement of patterns became less dense for younger princes. A controversy arose during the investiture of Crown Prince Hirohito (later {{ill|Emperor Shōwa|ja|昭和天皇}}), questioning whether the single mandarin duck should be replaced by a paired design or even by a crane motif. Ultimately, the original pattern was retained based on consultations with Kyoto noble families.

{{Infobox webcolor|title=Ōni (Crown Prince's Ceremonial Color)|hex=F47A55}} [[File:Prince Naruhito wearing Sokutai.jpg|thumb|200px|On [[November 12, 1990]], Crown Prince [[Naruhito]] wearing the Ōni robe at the [[2019 Japanese imperial transition|Sokutai ceremony]] for Emperor [[Akihito]].]] [[File:Emperor Naruhito and Empress Masako in formal wedding robes.jpg|thumb|300px|In [[1993]], the wedding ceremony of Crown Prince Naruhito and [[Masako Owada]]]] '''Ōni robe''' (黄丹袍, おうにのほう) is the {{ill|sokutai|ja|束帯}} attire worn by the {{ill|Crown Prince of Japan|ja|皇太子}} or {{ill|imperial heir|ja|皇嗣}} during ceremonial occasions. The name is derived from the color {{ill|Ōni|ja|黄丹}}, a shade achieved through a dyeing process using {{ill|Gardenia jasminoides|ja|クチナシ}} and {{ill|safflower|ja|紅花}}.

In contrast, the origins of Ōni in Japan date back to the {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老律令}} during the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}, where it was designated in the "Dress Code" for the Crown Prince’s ceremonial and formal robes. It is believed to continue the tradition of the {{ill|Hanezuiro|ja|朱華色}} lineage, unrelated to Chinese customs where early Tang princes wore purple robes.
Ōni symbolizes the rising sun, and its use was strictly reserved for the Crown Prince, designated as a {{ill|forbidden color|ja|禁色}}.
During the early Heian period, the Crown Prince’s ceremonial attire transitioned to red robes (九章服), and by the mid-Heian period, its use in court diminished. However, in formal dress (朝服), Ōni persisted, with {{ill|oshidori|ja|鴛鴦丸}} (mandarin duck) patterns becoming standardized.
By the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, the practice of aqqointing Crown Princes ceased, leading to a temporary discontinuation of the Ōni robe. The custom revived in the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}} with the investiture of Crown Princes like Prince Asahito (later {{ill|Emperor Higashiyama|ja|東山天皇}}). However, records suggest some Crown Princes initially wore reddish-brown robes called "red robes," a term potentially synonymous with Ōni.
In the {{ill|Reiwa era|ja|令和}}, the robe is worn by the imperial heir, {{ill|Fumihito, Prince Akishino|ja|秋篠宮文仁親王}}, during ceremonies.<ref>[https://www.sankei.com/article/2020qq08-GFKCPJRR3RJDHLW3MWBDGY3BAY/ "Ceremonial attire 'rising sun's color' specified in the Nara period"], ''Sankei News'', November 8, 2020.</ref>
== Design ==
Since the time of Prince Asahito, the robe’s crest has featured a flower-shaped frame with a downward-facing {{ill|oshidori|ja|鴛鴦}} (mandarin duck) motif. This design remains unchanged.
According to regulations from the late Edo period, under both the {{ill|Yamashina family|ja|山科家}} and {{ill|Takakura family|ja|高倉家}}, winter robes for mature Crown Princes used yellow silk linings, while younger princes wore matching yellow linings for their {{ill|Kettetsu|ja|闕腋袍}}. The placement of crests on the fabric adhered to a fixed grid without staggered alignment, similar to the Emperor’s robes.
The material and weaving methods evolved in modern times, but the arrangement of patterns became less dense for younger princes. A controversy arose during the investiture of Crown Prince Hirohito (later {{ill|Emperor Shōwa|ja|昭和天皇}}), questioning whether the single mandarin duck should be replaced by a paired design or even by a crane motif. Ultimately, the original pattern was retained based on consultations with Kyoto noble families.

{{Infobox webcolor|title=Ōni (Crown Prince's Ceremonial Color)|hex=F47A55}} [[File:Prince Naruhito wearing Sokutai.jpg|thumb|200px|On [[November 12, 1990]], Crown Prince [[Naruhito]] wearing the Ōni robe at the [[2019 Japanese imperial transition|Sokutai ceremony]] for Emperor [[Akihito]].]] [[File:Emperor Naruhito and Empress Masako in formal wedding robes.jpg|thumb|300px|In [[1993]], the wedding ceremony of Crown Prince Naruhito and [[Masako Owada]]]] '''Ōni robe''' (黄丹袍, おうにのほう) is the {{ill|sokutai|ja|束帯}} attire worn by the {{ill|Crown Prince of Japan|ja|皇太子}} or {{ill|imperial heir|ja|皇嗣}} during ceremonial occasions. The name is derived from the color {{ill|Ōni|ja|黄丹}}, a shade achieved through a dyeing process using {{ill|Gardenia jasminoides|ja|クチナシ}} and {{ill|safflower|ja|紅花}}.
== History ==
The {{ill|Kōrozen|ja|黄櫨染御袍}}, worn by the Emperor, originated in early {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} as an adaptation of the Chinese ceremonial garment {{ill|Sha Huang|zh|赭黄袍}}, replacing earlier white robes. In contrast, the origins of Ōni in Japan date back to the {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老律令}} during the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}, where it was designated in the "Dress Code" for the Crown Prince’s ceremonial and formal robes. It is believed to continue the tradition of the {{ill|Hanezuiro|ja|朱華色}} lineage, unrelated to Chinese customs where early Tang princes wore purple robes.
Ōni symbolizes the rising sun, and its use was strictly reserved for the Crown Prince, designated as a {{ill|forbidden color|ja|禁色}}.
During the early Heian period, the Crown Prince’s ceremonial attire transitioned to red robes (九章服), and by the mid-Heian period, its use in court diminished. However, in formal dress (朝服), Ōni persisted, with {{ill|oshidori|ja|鴛鴦丸}} (mandarin duck) patterns becoming standardized.
By the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, the practice of aqqointing Crown Princes ceased, leading to a temporary discontinuation of the Ōni robe. The custom revived in the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}} with the investiture of Crown Princes like Prince Asahito (later {{ill|Emperor Higashiyama|ja|東山天皇}}). However, records suggest some Crown Princes initially wore reddish-brown robes called "red robes," a term potentially synonymous with Ōni.
In the {{ill|Reiwa era|ja|令和}}, the robe is worn by the imperial heir, {{ill|Fumihito, Prince Akishino|ja|秋篠宮文仁親王}}, during ceremonies.<ref>[https://www.sankei.com/article/2020qq08-GFKCPJRR3RJDHLW3MWBDGY3BAY/ "Ceremonial attire 'rising sun's color' specified in the Nara period"], ''Sankei News'', November 8, 2020.</ref>
== Design ==
Since the time of Prince Asahito, the robe’s crest has featured a flower-shaped frame with a downward-facing {{ill|oshidori|ja|鴛鴦}} (mandarin duck) motif. This design remains unchanged.
According to regulations from the late Edo period, under both the {{ill|Yamashina family|ja|山科家}} and {{ill|Takakura family|ja|高倉家}}, winter robes for mature Crown Princes used yellow silk linings, while younger princes wore matching yellow linings for their {{ill|Kettetsu|ja|闕腋袍}}. The placement of crests on the fabric adhered to a fixed grid without staggered alignment, similar to the Emperor’s robes.
The material and weaving methods evolved in modern times, but the arrangement of patterns became less dense for younger princes. A controversy arose during the investiture of Crown Prince Hirohito (later {{ill|Emperor Shōwa|ja|昭和天皇}}), questioning whether the single mandarin duck should be replaced by a paired design or even by a crane motif. Ultimately, the original pattern was retained based on consultations with Kyoto noble families.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Kikujin robe|ja|麹塵袍}}

revision.text:
{{Infobox webcolor|title=Ōni (Crown Prince's Ceremonial Color)|hex=F47A55}} [[File:Prince Naruhito wearing Sokutai.jpg|thumb|200px|On [[November 12, 1990]], Crown Prince [[Naruhito]] wearing the Ōni robe at the [[2019 Japanese imperial transition|Sokutai ceremony]] for Emperor [[Akihito]].]] [[File:Emperor Naruhito and Empress Masako in formal wedding robes.jpg|thumb|300px|In [[1993]], the wedding ceremony of Crown Prince Naruhito and [[Masako Owada]]]] '''Ōni robe''' (黄丹袍, おうにのほう) is the {{ill|sokutai|ja|束帯}} attire worn by the {{ill|Crown Prince of Japan|ja|皇太子}} or {{ill|imperial heir|ja|皇嗣}} during ceremonial occasions. The name is derived from the color {{ill|Ōni|ja|黄丹}}, a shade achieved through a dyeing process using {{ill|Gardenia jasminoides|ja|クチナシ}} and {{ill|safflower|ja|紅花}}.
== History ==
The {{ill|Kōrozen|ja|黄櫨染御袍}}, worn by the Emperor, originated in early {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} as an adaptation of the Chinese ceremonial garment {{ill|Sha Huang|zh|赭黄袍}}, replacing earlier white robes. In contrast, the origins of Ōni in Japan date back to the {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老律令}} during the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}, where it was designated in the "Dress Code" for the Crown Prince’s ceremonial and formal robes. It is believed to continue the tradition of the {{ill|Hanezuiro|ja|朱華色}} lineage, unrelated to Chinese customs where early Tang princes wore purple robes.
Ōni symbolizes the rising sun, and its use was strictly reserved for the Crown Prince, designated as a {{ill|forbidden color|ja|禁色}}.
During the early Heian period, the Crown Prince’s ceremonial attire transitioned to red robes (九章服), and by the mid-Heian period, its use in court diminished. However, in formal dress (朝服), Ōni persisted, with {{ill|oshidori|ja|鴛鴦丸}} (mandarin duck) patterns becoming standardized.
By the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, the practice of a11ointing Crown Princes ceased, leading to a temporary discontinuation of the Ōni robe. The custom revived in the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}} with the investiture of Crown Princes like Prince Asahito (later {{ill|Emperor Higashiyama|ja|東山天皇}}). However, records suggest some Crown Princes initially wore reddish-brown robes called "red robes," a term potentially synonymous with Ōni.
In the {{ill|Reiwa era|ja|令和}}, the robe is worn by the imperial heir, {{ill|Fumihito, Prince Akishino|ja|秋篠宮文仁親王}}, during ceremonies.<ref>[https://www.sankei.com/article/20201108-GFKCPJRR3RJDHLW3MWBDGY3BAY/ "Ceremonial attire 'rising sun's color' specified in the Nara period"], ''Sankei News'', November 8, 2020.</ref>
== Design ==
Since the time of Prince Asahito, the robe’s crest has featured a flower-shaped frame with a downward-facing {{ill|oshidori|ja|鴛鴦}} (mandarin duck) motif. This design remains unchanged.
According to regulations from the late Edo period, under both the {{ill|Yamashina family|ja|山科家}} and {{ill|Takakura family|ja|高倉家}}, winter robes for mature Crown Princes used yellow silk linings, while younger princes wore matching yellow linings for their {{ill|Kettetsu|ja|闕腋袍}}. The placement of crests on the fabric adhered to a fixed grid without staggered alignment, similar to the Emperor’s robes.
The material and weaving methods evolved in modern times, but the arrangement of patterns became less dense for younger princes. A controversy arose during the investiture of Crown Prince Hirohito (later {{ill|Emperor Shōwa|ja|昭和天皇}}), questioning whether the single mandarin duck should be replaced by a paired design or even by a crane motif. Ultimately, the original pattern was retained based on consultations with Kyoto noble families.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Kikujin robe|ja|麹塵袍}}
== References == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
== External Links == * [http://yuusoku.jp/ Yūsoku Culture Research Institute] * [http://www.iz2.or.jp/ Costume Museum]

It is believed to continue the tradition of the {{ill|Hanezuiro|ja|朱華色}} lineage, unrelated to Chinese customs where early Tang princes wore purple robes.
Ōni symbolizes the rising sun, and its use was strictly reserved for the Crown Prince, designated as a {{ill|forbidden color|ja|禁色}}.
During the early Heian period, the Crown Prince’s ceremonial attire transitioned to red robes (九章服), and by the mid-Heian period, its use in court diminished. However, in formal dress (朝服), Ōni persisted, with {{ill|oshidori|ja|鴛鴦丸}} (mandarin duck) patterns becoming standardized.
By the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, the practice of a11ointing Crown Princes ceased, leading to a temporary discontinuation of the Ōni robe. The custom revived in the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}} with the investiture of Crown Princes like Prince Asahito (later {{ill|Emperor Higashiyama|ja|東山天皇}}). However, records suggest some Crown Princes initially wore reddish-brown robes called "red robes," a term potentially synonymous with Ōni.
In the {{ill|Reiwa era|ja|令和}}, the robe is worn by the imperial heir, {{ill|Fumihito, Prince Akishino|ja|秋篠宮文仁親王}}, during ceremonies.<ref>[https://www.sankei.com/article/20201108-GFKCPJRR3RJDHLW3MWBDGY3BAY/ "Ceremonial attire 'rising sun's color' specified in the Nara period"], ''Sankei News'', November 8, 2020.</ref>
== Design ==
Since the time of Prince Asahito, the robe’s crest has featured a flower-shaped frame with a downward-facing {{ill|oshidori|ja|鴛鴦}} (mandarin duck) motif. This design remains unchanged.
According to regulations from the late Edo period, under both the {{ill|Yamashina family|ja|山科家}} and {{ill|Takakura family|ja|高倉家}}, winter robes for mature Crown Princes used yellow silk linings, while younger princes wore matching yellow linings for their {{ill|Kettetsu|ja|闕腋袍}}. The placement of crests on the fabric adhered to a fixed grid without staggered alignment, similar to the Emperor’s robes.
The material and weaving methods evolved in modern times, but the arrangement of patterns became less dense for younger princes. A controversy arose during the investiture of Crown Prince Hirohito (later {{ill|Emperor Shōwa|ja|昭和天皇}}), questioning whether the single mandarin duck should be replaced by a paired design or even by a crane motif. Ultimately, the original pattern was retained based on consultations with Kyoto noble families.

{{Infobox webcolor|title=Ōni (Crown Prince's Ceremonial Color)|hex=F47A55}} [[File:Prince Naruhito wearing Sokutai.jpg|thumb|200px|On [[November 12, 1990]], Crown Prince [[Naruhito]] wearing the Ōni robe at the [[2019 Japanese imperial transition|Sokutai ceremony]] for Emperor [[Akihito]].]] [[File:Emperor Naruhito and Empress Masako in formal wedding robes.jpg|thumb|300px|In [[1993]], the wedding ceremony of Crown Prince Naruhito and [[Masako Owada]]]] '''Ōni robe''' (黄丹袍, おうにのほう) is the {{ill|sokutai|ja|束帯}} attire worn by the {{ill|Crown Prince of Japan|ja|皇太子}} or {{ill|imperial heir|ja|皇嗣}} during ceremonial occasions. The name is derived from the color {{ill|Ōni|ja|黄丹}}, a shade achieved through a dyeing process using {{ill|Gardenia jasminoides|ja|クチナシ}} and {{ill|safflower|ja|紅花}}.
== History ==
The {{ill|Kōrozen|ja|黄櫨染御袍}}, worn by the Emperor, originated in early {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} as an adaptation of the Chinese ceremonial garment {{ill|Sha Huang|zh|赭黄袍}}, replacing earlier white robes. In contrast, the origins of Ōni in Japan date back to the {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老律令}} during the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}, where it was designated in the "Dress Code" for the Crown Prince’s ceremonial and formal robes. It is believed to continue the tradition of the {{ill|Hanezuiro|ja|朱華色}} lineage, unrelated to Chinese customs where early Tang princes wore purple robes.
Ōni symbolizes the rising sun, and its use was strictly reserved for the Crown Prince, designated as a {{ill|forbidden color|ja|禁色}}.
During the early Heian period, the Crown Prince’s ceremonial attire transitioned to red robes (九章服), and by the mid-Heian period, its use in court diminished. However, in formal dress (朝服), Ōni persisted, with {{ill|oshidori|ja|鴛鴦丸}} (mandarin duck) patterns becoming standardized.
By the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, the practice of a11ointing Crown Princes ceased, leading to a temporary discontinuation of the Ōni robe. The custom revived in the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}} with the investiture of Crown Princes like Prince Asahito (later {{ill|Emperor Higashiyama|ja|東山天皇}}). However, records suggest some Crown Princes initially wore reddish-brown robes called "red robes," a term potentially synonymous with Ōni.
In the {{ill|Reiwa era|ja|令和}}, the robe is worn by the imperial heir, {{ill|Fumihito, Prince Akishino|ja|秋篠宮文仁親王}}, during ceremonies.<ref>[https://www.sankei.com/article/20201108-GFKCPJRR3RJDHLW3MWBDGY3BAY/ "Ceremonial attire 'rising sun's color' specified in the Nara period"], ''Sankei News'', November 8, 2020.</ref>
== Design ==
Since the time of Prince Asahito, the robe’s crest has featured a flower-shaped frame with a downward-facing {{ill|oshidori|ja|鴛鴦}} (mandarin duck) motif. This design remains unchanged.
According to regulations from the late Edo period, under both the {{ill|Yamashina family|ja|山科家}} and {{ill|Takakura family|ja|高倉家}}, winter robes for mature Crown Princes used yellow silk linings, while younger princes wore matching yellow linings for their {{ill|Kettetsu|ja|闕腋袍}}. The placement of crests on the fabric adhered to a fixed grid without staggered alignment, similar to the Emperor’s robes.
The material and weaving methods evolved in modern times, but the arrangement of patterns became less dense for younger princes. A controversy arose during the investiture of Crown Prince Hirohito (later {{ill|Emperor Shōwa|ja|昭和天皇}}), questioning whether the single mandarin duck should be replaced by a paired design or even by a crane motif. Ultimately, the original pattern was retained based on consultations with Kyoto noble families.

{{Infobox webcolor|title=Ōni (Crown Prince's Ceremonial Color)|hex=F47A55}} [[File:Prince Naruhito wearing Sokutai.jpg|thumb|200px|On [[November 12, 1990]], Crown Prince [[Naruhito]] wearing the Ōni robe at the [[2019 Japanese imperial transition|Sokutai ceremony]] for Emperor [[Akihito]].]] [[File:Emperor Naruhito and Empress Masako in formal wedding robes.jpg|thumb|300px|In [[1993]], the wedding ceremony of Crown Prince Naruhito and [[Masako Owada]]]] '''Ōni robe''' (黄丹袍, おうにのほう) is the {{ill|sokutai|ja|束帯}} attire worn by the {{ill|Crown Prince of Japan|ja|皇太子}} or {{ill|imperial heir|ja|皇嗣}} during ceremonial occasions. The name is derived from the color {{ill|Ōni|ja|黄丹}}, a shade achieved through a dyeing process using {{ill|Gardenia jasminoides|ja|クチナシ}} and {{ill|safflower|ja|紅花}}.
== History ==
The {{ill|Kōrozen|ja|黄櫨染御袍}}, worn by the Emperor, originated in early {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} as an adaptation of the Chinese ceremonial garment {{ill|Sha Huang|zh|赭黄袍}}, replacing earlier white robes. In contrast, the origins of Ōni in Japan date back to the {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老律令}} during the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}, where it was designated in the "Dress Code" for the Crown Prince’s ceremonial and formal robes. It is believed to continue the tradition of the {{ill|Hanezuiro|ja|朱華色}} lineage, unrelated to Chinese customs where early Tang princes wore purple robes.
Ōni symbolizes the rising sun, and its use was strictly reserved for the Crown Prince, designated as a {{ill|forbidden color|ja|禁色}}.
During the early Heian period, the Crown Prince’s ceremonial attire transitioned to red robes (九章服), and by the mid-Heian period, its use in court diminished. However, in formal dress (朝服), Ōni persisted, with {{ill|oshidori|ja|鴛鴦丸}} (mandarin duck) patterns becoming standardized.
By the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, the practice of a11ointing Crown Princes ceased, leading to a temporary discontinuation of the Ōni robe. The custom revived in the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}} with the investiture of Crown Princes like Prince Asahito (later {{ill|Emperor Higashiyama|ja|東山天皇}}). However, records suggest some Crown Princes initially wore reddish-brown robes called "red robes," a term potentially synonymous with Ōni.
In the {{ill|Reiwa era|ja|令和}}, the robe is worn by the imperial heir, {{ill|Fumihito, Prince Akishino|ja|秋篠宮文仁親王}}, during ceremonies.<ref>[https://www.sankei.com/article/20201108-GFKCPJRR3RJDHLW3MWBDGY3BAY/ "Ceremonial attire 'rising sun's color' specified in the Nara period"], ''Sankei News'', November 8, 2020.</ref>
== Design ==
Since the time of Prince Asahito, the robe’s crest has featured a flower-shaped frame with a downward-facing {{ill|oshidori|ja|鴛鴦}} (mandarin duck) motif. This design remains unchanged.
According to regulations from the late Edo period, under both the {{ill|Yamashina family|ja|山科家}} and {{ill|Takakura family|ja|高倉家}}, winter robes for mature Crown Princes used yellow silk linings, while younger princes wore matching yellow linings for their {{ill|Kettetsu|ja|闕腋袍}}. The placement of crests on the fabric adhered to a fixed grid without staggered alignment, similar to the Emperor’s robes.
The material and weaving methods evolved in modern times, but the arrangement of patterns became less dense for younger princes. A controversy arose during the investiture of Crown Prince Hirohito (later {{ill|Emperor Shōwa|ja|昭和天皇}}), questioning whether the single mandarin duck should be replaced by a paired design or even by a crane motif. Ultimately, the original pattern was retained based on consultations with Kyoto noble families.

The custom revived in the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}} with the investiture of Crown Princes like Prince Asahito (later {{ill|Emperor Higashiyama|ja|東山天皇}}). However, records suggest some Crown Princes initially wore reddish-brown robes called "red robes," a term potentially synonymous with Ōni.
In the {{ill|Reiwa era|ja|令和}}, the robe is worn by the imperial heir, {{ill|Fumihito, Prince Akishino|ja|秋篠宮文仁親王}}, during ceremonies.<ref>[https://www.sankei.com/article/20201108-GFKCPJRR3RJDHLW3MWBDGY3BAY/ "Ceremonial attire 'rising sun's color' specified in the Nara period"], ''Sankei News'', November 8, 2020.</ref>
== Design ==
Since the time of Prince Asahito, the robe’s crest has featured a flower-shaped frame with a downward-facing {{ill|oshidori|ja|鴛鴦}} (mandarin duck) motif. This design remains unchanged.
According to regulations from the late Edo period, under both the {{ill|Yamashina family|ja|山科家}} and {{ill|Takakura family|ja|高倉家}}, winter robes for mature Crown Princes used yellow silk linings, while younger princes wore matching yellow linings for their {{ill|Kettetsu|ja|闕腋袍}}. The placement of crests on the fabric adhered to a fixed grid without staggered alignment, similar to the Emperor’s robes.
The material and weaving methods evolved in modern times, but the arrangement of patterns became less dense for younger princes. A controversy arose during the investiture of Crown Prince Hirohito (later {{ill|Emperor Shōwa|ja|昭和天皇}}), questioning whether the single mandarin duck should be replaced by a paired design or even by a crane motif. Ultimately, the original pattern was retained based on consultations with Kyoto noble families.

{{Infobox webcolor|title=Ōni (Crown Prince's Ceremonial Color)|hex=F47A55}} [[File:Prince Naruhito wearing Sokutai.jpg|thumb|200px|On [[November 12, 1990]], Crown Prince [[Naruhito]] wearing the Ōni robe at the [[2019 Japanese imperial transition|Sokutai ceremony]] for Emperor [[Akihito]].]] [[File:Emperor Naruhito and Empress Masako in formal wedding robes.jpg|thumb|300px|In [[1993]], the wedding ceremony of Crown Prince Naruhito and [[Masako Owada]]]] '''Ōni robe''' (黄丹袍, おうにのほう) is the {{ill|sokutai|ja|束帯}} attire worn by the {{ill|Crown Prince of Japan|ja|皇太子}} or {{ill|imperial heir|ja|皇嗣}} during ceremonial occasions. The name is derived from the color {{ill|Ōni|ja|黄丹}}, a shade achieved through a dyeing process using {{ill|Gardenia jasminoides|ja|クチナシ}} and {{ill|safflower|ja|紅花}}.
== History ==
The {{ill|Kōrozen|ja|黄櫨染御袍}}, worn by the Emperor, originated in early {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} as an adaptation of the Chinese ceremonial garment {{ill|Sha Huang|zh|赭黄袍}}, replacing earlier white robes. In contrast, the origins of Ōni in Japan date back to the {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老律令}} during the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}, where it was designated in the "Dress Code" for the Crown Prince’s ceremonial and formal robes. It is believed to continue the tradition of the {{ill|Hanezuiro|ja|朱華色}} lineage, unrelated to Chinese customs where early Tang princes wore purple robes.
Ōni symbolizes the rising sun, and its use was strictly reserved for the Crown Prince, designated as a {{ill|forbidden color|ja|禁色}}.
During the early Heian period, the Crown Prince’s ceremonial attire transitioned to red robes (九章服), and by the mid-Heian period, its use in court diminished. However, in formal dress (朝服), Ōni persisted, with {{ill|oshidori|ja|鴛鴦丸}} (mandarin duck) patterns becoming standardized.
By the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, the practice of a11ointing Crown Princes ceased, leading to a temporary discontinuation of the Ōni robe. The custom revived in the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}} with the investiture of Crown Princes like Prince Asahito (later {{ill|Emperor Higashiyama|ja|東山天皇}}). However, records suggest some Crown Princes initially wore reddish-brown robes called "red robes," a term potentially synonymous with Ōni.
In the {{ill|Reiwa era|ja|令和}}, the robe is worn by the imperial heir, {{ill|Fumihito, Prince Akishino|ja|秋篠宮文仁親王}}, during ceremonies.<ref>[https://www.sankei.com/article/20201108-GFKCPJRR3RJDHLW3MWBDGY3BAY/ "Ceremonial attire 'rising sun's color' specified in the Nara period"], ''Sankei News'', November 8, 2020.</ref>
== Design ==
Since the time of Prince Asahito, the robe’s crest has featured a flower-shaped frame with a downward-facing {{ill|oshidori|ja|鴛鴦}} (mandarin duck) motif. This design remains unchanged.
According to regulations from the late Edo period, under both the {{ill|Yamashina family|ja|山科家}} and {{ill|Takakura family|ja|高倉家}}, winter robes for mature Crown Princes used yellow silk linings, while younger princes wore matching yellow linings for their {{ill|Kettetsu|ja|闕腋袍}}. The placement of crests on the fabric adhered to a fixed grid without staggered alignment, similar to the Emperor’s robes.
The material and weaving methods evolved in modern times, but the arrangement of patterns became less dense for younger princes. A controversy arose during the investiture of Crown Prince Hirohito (later {{ill|Emperor Shōwa|ja|昭和天皇}}), questioning whether the single mandarin duck should be replaced by a paired design or even by a crane motif. Ultimately, the original pattern was retained based on consultations with Kyoto noble families.

{{Infobox webcolor|title=Ōni (Crown Prince's Ceremonial Color)|hex=F47A55}} [[File:Prince Naruhito wearing Sokutai.jpg|thumb|200px|On [[November 12, 1990]], Crown Prince [[Naruhito]] wearing the Ōni robe at the [[2019 Japanese imperial transition|Sokutai ceremony]] for Emperor [[Akihito]].]] [[File:Emperor Naruhito and Empress Masako in formal wedding robes.jpg|thumb|300px|In [[1993]], the wedding ceremony of Crown Prince Naruhito and [[Masako Owada]]]] '''Ōni robe''' (黄丹袍, おうにのほう) is the {{ill|sokutai|ja|束帯}} attire worn by the {{ill|Crown Prince of Japan|ja|皇太子}} or {{ill|imperial heir|ja|皇嗣}} during ceremonial occasions. The name is derived from the color {{ill|Ōni|ja|黄丹}}, a shade achieved through a dyeing process using {{ill|Gardenia jasminoides|ja|クチナシ}} and {{ill|safflower|ja|紅花}}.
== History ==
The {{ill|Kōrozen|ja|黄櫨染御袍}}, worn by the Emperor, originated in early {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} as an adaptation of the Chinese ceremonial garment {{ill|Sha Huang|zh|赭黄袍}}, replacing earlier white robes. In contrast, the origins of Ōni in Japan date back to the {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老律令}} during the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}, where it was designated in the "Dress Code" for the Crown Prince’s ceremonial and formal robes. It is believed to continue the tradition of the {{ill|Hanezuiro|ja|朱華色}} lineage, unrelated to Chinese customs where early Tang princes wore purple robes.
Ōni symbolizes the rising sun, and its use was strictly reserved for the Crown Prince, designated as a {{ill|forbidden color|ja|禁色}}.
During the early Heian period, the Crown Prince’s ceremonial attire transitioned to red robes (九章服), and by the mid-Heian period, its use in court diminished. However, in formal dress (朝服), Ōni persisted, with {{ill|oshidori|ja|鴛鴦丸}} (mandarin duck) patterns becoming standardized.
By the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, the practice of a11ointing Crown Princes ceased, leading to a temporary discontinuation of the Ōni robe. The custom revived in the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}} with the investiture of Crown Princes like Prince Asahito (later {{ill|Emperor Higashiyama|ja|東山天皇}}). However, records suggest some Crown Princes initially wore reddish-brown robes called "red robes," a term potentially synonymous with Ōni.
In the {{ill|Reiwa era|ja|令和}}, the robe is worn by the imperial heir, {{ill|Fumihito, Prince Akishino|ja|秋篠宮文仁親王}}, during ceremonies.<ref>[https://www.sankei.com/article/20201108-GFKCPJRR3RJDHLW3MWBDGY3BAY/ "Ceremonial attire 'rising sun's color' specified in the Nara period"], ''Sankei News'', November 8, 2020.</ref>
== Design ==
Since the time of Prince Asahito, the robe’s crest has featured a flower-shaped frame with a downward-facing {{ill|oshidori|ja|鴛鴦}} (mandarin duck) motif. This design remains unchanged.
According to regulations from the late Edo period, under both the {{ill|Yamashina family|ja|山科家}} and {{ill|Takakura family|ja|高倉家}}, winter robes for mature Crown Princes used yellow silk linings, while younger princes wore matching yellow linings for their {{ill|Kettetsu|ja|闕腋袍}}. The placement of crests on the fabric adhered to a fixed grid without staggered alignment, similar to the Emperor’s robes.
The material and weaving methods evolved in modern times, but the arrangement of patterns became less dense for younger princes. A controversy arose during the investiture of Crown Prince Hirohito (later {{ill|Emperor Shōwa|ja|昭和天皇}}), questioning whether the single mandarin duck should be replaced by a paired design or even by a crane motif. Ultimately, the original pattern was retained based on consultations with Kyoto noble families.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Kikujin robe|ja|麹塵袍}}
== References ==
{{Reflist}}
== External Links == * [http://yuusoku.jp/ Yūsoku Culture Research Institute] * [http://www.iz2.or.jp/ Costume Museum]

It is believed to continue the tradition of the {{ill|Hanezuiro|ja|朱華色|12=draft}} lineage, unrelated to Chinese customs where early Tang princes wore purple robes.
Ōni symbolizes the rising sun, and its use was strictly reserved for the Crown Prince, designated as a {{ill|forbidden color|ja|禁色|12=draft}}.
During the early Heian period, the Crown Prince’s ceremonial attire transitioned to red robes (九章服), and by the mid-Heian period, its use in court diminished. However, in formal dress (朝服), Ōni persisted, with {{ill|oshidori|ja|鴛鴦丸|12=draft}} (mandarin duck) patterns becoming standardized.
By the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代|12=draft}}, the practice of a11ointing Crown Princes ceased, leading to a temporary discontinuation of the Ōni robe. The custom revived in the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|12=draft}} with the investiture of Crown Princes like Prince Asahito (later {{ill|Emperor Higashiyama|ja|東山天皇|12=draft}}). However, records suggest some Crown Princes initially wore reddish-brown robes called "red robes," a term potentially synonymous with Ōni.
In the {{ill|Reiwa era|ja|令和|12=draft}}, the robe is worn by the imperial heir, {{ill|Fumihito, Prince Akishino|ja|秋篠宮文仁親王|12=draft}}, during ceremonies.<ref>[https://www.sankei.com/article/20201108-GFKCPJRR3RJDHLW3MWBDGY3BAY/ "Ceremonial attire 'rising sun's color' specified in the Nara period"], ''Sankei News'', November 8, 2020.</ref>
== Design ==
Since the time of Prince Asahito, the robe’s crest has featured a flower-shaped frame with a downward-facing {{ill|oshidori|ja|鴛鴦|12=draft}} (mandarin duck) motif. This design remains unchanged.
According to regulations from the late Edo period, under both the {{ill|Yamashina family|ja|山科家|12=draft}} and {{ill|Takakura family|ja|高倉家|12=draft}}, winter robes for mature Crown Princes used yellow silk linings, while younger princes wore matching yellow linings for their {{ill|Kettetsu|ja|闕腋袍|12=draft}}. The placement of crests on the fabric adhered to a fixed grid without staggered alignment, similar to the Emperor’s robes.
The material and weaving methods evolved in modern times, but the arrangement of patterns became less dense for younger princes. A controversy arose during the investiture of Crown Prince Hirohito (later {{ill|Emperor Shōwa|ja|昭和天皇|12=draft}}), questioning whether the single mandarin duck should be replaced by a paired design or even by a crane motif. Ultimately, the original pattern was retained based on consultations with Kyoto noble families.

{{Infobox webcolor|title=Ōni (Crown Prince's Ceremonial Color)|hex=F47A55}} [[File:Prince Naruhito wearing Sokutai.jpg|thumb|200px|On [[November 12, 1990]], Crown Prince [[Naruhito]] wearing the Ōni robe at the [[2019 Japanese imperial transition|Sokutai ceremony]] for Emperor [[Akihito]].]] [[File:Emperor Naruhito and Empress Masako in formal wedding robes.jpg|thumb|300px|In [[1993]], the wedding ceremony of Crown Prince Naruhito and [[Masako Owada]]]] '''Ōni robe''' (黄丹袍, おうにのほう) is the {{ill|sokutai|ja|束帯|12=draft}} attire worn by the {{ill|Crown Prince of Japan|ja|皇太子|12=draft}} or {{ill|imperial heir|ja|皇嗣|12=draft}} during ceremonial occasions. The name is derived from the color {{ill|Ōni|ja|黄丹|12=draft}}, a shade achieved through a dyeing process using {{ill|Gardenia jasminoides|ja|クチナシ|12=draft}} and {{ill|safflower|ja|紅花|12=draft}}.
== History ==
The {{ill|Kōrozen|ja|黄櫨染御袍|12=draft}}, worn by the Emperor, originated in early {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代|12=draft}} as an adaptation of the Chinese ceremonial garment {{ill|Sha Huang|zh|赭黄袍|12=draft}}, replacing earlier white robes. In contrast, the origins of Ōni in Japan date back to the {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老律令|12=draft}} during the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代|12=draft}}, where it was designated in the "Dress Code" for the Crown Prince’s ceremonial and formal robes. It is believed to continue the tradition of the {{ill|Hanezuiro|ja|朱華色|12=draft}} lineage, unrelated to Chinese customs where early Tang princes wore purple robes.
Ōni symbolizes the rising sun, and its use was strictly reserved for the Crown Prince, designated as a {{ill|forbidden color|ja|禁色|12=draft}}.
During the early Heian period, the Crown Prince’s ceremonial attire transitioned to red robes (九章服), and by the mid-Heian period, its use in court diminished. However, in formal dress (朝服), Ōni persisted, with {{ill|oshidori|ja|鴛鴦丸|12=draft}} (mandarin duck) patterns becoming standardized.
By the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代|12=draft}}, the practice of a11ointing Crown Princes ceased, leading to a temporary discontinuation of the Ōni robe. The custom revived in the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|12=draft}} with the investiture of Crown Princes like Prince Asahito (later {{ill|Emperor Higashiyama|ja|東山天皇|12=draft}}). However, records suggest some Crown Princes initially wore reddish-brown robes called "red robes," a term potentially synonymous with Ōni.
In the {{ill|Reiwa era|ja|令和|12=draft}}, the robe is worn by the imperial heir, {{ill|Fumihito, Prince Akishino|ja|秋篠宮文仁親王|12=draft}}, during ceremonies.<ref>[https://www.sankei.com/article/20201108-GFKCPJRR3RJDHLW3MWBDGY3BAY/ "Ceremonial attire 'rising sun's color' specified in the Nara period"], ''Sankei News'', November 8, 2020.</ref>
== Design ==
Since the time of Prince Asahito, the robe’s crest has featured a flower-shaped frame with a downward-facing {{ill|oshidori|ja|鴛鴦|12=draft}} (mandarin duck) motif. This design remains unchanged.
According to regulations from the late Edo period, under both the {{ill|Yamashina family|ja|山科家|12=draft}} and {{ill|Takakura family|ja|高倉家|12=draft}}, winter robes for mature Crown Princes used yellow silk linings, while younger princes wore matching yellow linings for their {{ill|Kettetsu|ja|闕腋袍|12=draft}}. The placement of crests on the fabric adhered to a fixed grid without staggered alignment, similar to the Emperor’s robes.
The material and weaving methods evolved in modern times, but the arrangement of patterns became less dense for younger princes. A controversy arose during the investiture of Crown Prince Hirohito (later {{ill|Emperor Shōwa|ja|昭和天皇|12=draft}}), questioning whether the single mandarin duck should be replaced by a paired design or even by a crane motif. Ultimately, the original pattern was retained based on consultations with Kyoto noble families.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Kikujin robe|ja|麹塵袍|12=draft}}

{{Reflist}}

In the {{ill|Reiwa era|ja|令和|12=draft}}, the robe is worn by the imperial heir, {{ill|Fumihito, Prince Akishino|ja|秋篠宮文仁親王|12=draft}}, during ceremonies.<ref>[https://www.sankei.com/article/20201108-GFKCPJRR3RJDHLW3MWBDGY3BAY/ "Ceremonial attire 'rising sun's color' specified in the Nara period"], ''Sankei News'', November 8, 2020.</ref>
== Design ==
Since the time of Prince Asahito, the robe’s crest has featured a flower-shaped frame with a downward-facing {{ill|oshidori|ja|鴛鴦|12=draft}} (mandarin duck) motif. This design remains unchanged.
According to regulations from the late Edo period, under both the {{ill|Yamashina family|ja|山科家|12=draft}} and {{ill|Takakura family|ja|高倉家|12=draft}}, winter robes for mature Crown Princes used yellow silk linings, while younger princes wore matching yellow linings for their {{ill|Kettetsu|ja|闕腋袍|12=draft}}. The placement of crests on the fabric adhered to a fixed grid without staggered alignment, similar to the Emperor’s robes.
The material and weaving methods evolved in modern times, but the arrangement of patterns became less dense for younger princes. A controversy arose during the investiture of Crown Prince Hirohito (later {{ill|Emperor Shōwa|ja|昭和天皇|12=draft}}), questioning whether the single mandarin duck should be replaced by a paired design or even by a crane motif. Ultimately, the original pattern was retained based on consultations with Kyoto noble families.

{{Infobox webcolor|title=Ōni (Crown Prince's Ceremonial Color)|hex=F47A55}} [[File:Prince Naruhito wearing Sokutai.jpg|thumb|200px|On [[November 12, 1990]], Crown Prince [[Naruhito]] wearing the Ōni robe at the [[2019 Japanese imperial transition|Sokutai ceremony]] for Emperor [[Akihito]].]] [[File:Emperor Naruhito and Empress Masako in formal wedding robes.jpg|thumb|300px|In [[1993]], the wedding ceremony of Crown Prince Naruhito and [[Masako Owada]]]] '''Ōni robe''' (黄丹袍, おうにのほう) is the {{ill|sokutai|ja|束帯|12=draft}} attire worn by the {{ill|Crown Prince of Japan|ja|皇太子|12=draft}} or {{ill|imperial heir|ja|皇嗣|12=draft}} during ceremonial occasions. The name is derived from the color {{ill|Ōni|ja|黄丹|12=draft}}, a shade achieved through a dyeing process using {{ill|Gardenia jasminoides|ja|クチナシ|12=draft}} and {{ill|safflower|ja|紅花|12=draft}}.
== History ==
The {{ill|Kōrozen|ja|黄櫨染御袍|12=draft}}, worn by the Emperor, originated in early {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代|12=draft}} as an adaptation of the Chinese ceremonial garment {{ill|Sha Huang|zh|赭黄袍|12=draft}}, replacing earlier white robes. In contrast, the origins of Ōni in Japan date back to the {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老律令|12=draft}} during the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代|12=draft}}, where it was designated in the "Dress Code" for the Crown Prince’s ceremonial and formal robes. It is believed to continue the tradition of the {{ill|Hanezuiro|ja|朱華色|12=draft}} lineage, unrelated to Chinese customs where early Tang princes wore purple robes.
Ōni symbolizes the rising sun, and its use was strictly reserved for the Crown Prince, designated as a {{ill|forbidden color|ja|禁色|12=draft}}.
During the early Heian period, the Crown Prince’s ceremonial attire transitioned to red robes (九章服), and by the mid-Heian period, its use in court diminished. However, in formal dress (朝服), Ōni persisted, with {{ill|oshidori|ja|鴛鴦丸|12=draft}} (mandarin duck) patterns becoming standardized.
By the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代|12=draft}}, the practice of a11ointing Crown Princes ceased, leading to a temporary discontinuation of the Ōni robe. The custom revived in the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|12=draft}} with the investiture of Crown Princes like Prince Asahito (later {{ill|Emperor Higashiyama|ja|東山天皇|12=draft}}). However, records suggest some Crown Princes initially wore reddish-brown robes called "red robes," a term potentially synonymous with Ōni.
In the {{ill|Reiwa era|ja|令和|12=draft}}, the robe is worn by the imperial heir, {{ill|Fumihito, Prince Akishino|ja|秋篠宮文仁親王|12=draft}}, during ceremonies.<ref>[https://www.sankei.com/article/20201108-GFKCPJRR3RJDHLW3MWBDGY3BAY/ "Ceremonial attire 'rising sun's color' specified in the Nara period"], ''Sankei News'', November 8, 2020.</ref>
== Design ==
Since the time of Prince Asahito, the robe’s crest has featured a flower-shaped frame with a downward-facing {{ill|oshidori|ja|鴛鴦|12=draft}} (mandarin duck) motif. This design remains unchanged.
According to regulations from the late Edo period, under both the {{ill|Yamashina family|ja|山科家|12=draft}} and {{ill|Takakura family|ja|高倉家|12=draft}}, winter robes for mature Crown Princes used yellow silk linings, while younger princes wore matching yellow linings for their {{ill|Kettetsu|ja|闕腋袍|12=draft}}. The placement of crests on the fabric adhered to a fixed grid without staggered alignment, similar to the Emperor’s robes.
The material and weaving methods evolved in modern times, but the arrangement of patterns became less dense for younger princes. A controversy arose during the investiture of Crown Prince Hirohito (later {{ill|Emperor Shōwa|ja|昭和天皇|12=draft}}), questioning whether the single mandarin duck should be replaced by a paired design or even by a crane motif. Ultimately, the original pattern was retained based on consultations with Kyoto noble families.

Since the time of Prince Asahito, the robe’s crest has featured a flower-shaped frame with a downward-facing {{ill|oshidori|ja|鴛鴦|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (mandarin duck) motif. This design remains unchanged.
According to regulations from the late Edo period, under both the {{ill|Yamashina family|ja|山科家|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Takakura family|ja|高倉家|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, winter robes for mature Crown Princes used yellow silk linings, while younger princes wore matching yellow linings for their {{ill|Kettetsu|ja|闕腋袍|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. The placement of crests on the fabric adhered to a fixed grid without staggered alignment, similar to the Emperor’s robes.
The material and weaving methods evolved in modern times, but the arrangement of patterns became less dense for younger princes. A controversy arose during the investiture of Crown Prince Hirohito (later {{ill|Emperor Shōwa|ja|昭和天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft}}), questioning whether the single mandarin duck should be replaced by a paired design or even by a crane motif. Ultimately, the original pattern was retained based on consultations with Kyoto noble families.

{{Infobox webcolor|title=Ōni (Crown Prince's Ceremonial Color)|hex=F47A55}} [[File:Prince Naruhito wearing Sokutai.jpg|thumb|200px|On [[November 12, 1990]], Crown Prince [[Naruhito]] wearing the Ōni robe at the [[2019 Japanese imperial transition|Sokutai ceremony]] for Emperor [[Akihito]].]] [[File:Emperor Naruhito and Empress Masako in formal wedding robes.jpg|thumb|300px|In [[1993]], the wedding ceremony of Crown Prince Naruhito and [[Masako Owada]]]] '''Ōni robe''' (黄丹袍, おうにのほう) is the {{ill|sokutai|ja|束帯|qq=draft|qq=draft}} attire worn by the {{ill|Crown Prince of Japan|ja|皇太子|qq=draft|qq=draft}} or {{ill|imperial heir|ja|皇嗣|qq=draft|qq=draft}} during ceremonial occasions. The name is derived from the color {{ill|Ōni|ja|黄丹|1=Ōdan|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, a shade achieved through a dyeing process using {{ill|Gardenia jasminoides|ja|クチナシ|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|safflower|ja|紅花|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
== History ==
The {{ill|Kōrozen|ja|黄櫨染御袍|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, worn by the Emperor, originated in early {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}} as an adaptation of the Chinese ceremonial garment {{ill|Sha Huang|zh|赭黄袍|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, replacing earlier white robes. In contrast, the origins of Ōni in Japan date back to the {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老律令|qq=draft|qq=draft}} during the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, where it was designated in the "Dress Code" for the Crown Prince’s ceremonial and formal robes. It is believed to continue the tradition of the {{ill|Hanezuiro|ja|朱華色|qq=draft|qq=draft}} lineage, unrelated to Chinese customs where early Tang princes wore purple robes.
Ōni symbolizes the rising sun, and its use was strictly reserved for the Crown Prince, designated as a {{ill|forbidden color|ja|禁色|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
During the early Heian period, the Crown Prince’s ceremonial attire transitioned to red robes (九章服), and by the mid-Heian period, its use in court diminished. However, in formal dress (朝服), Ōni persisted, with {{ill|oshidori|ja|鴛鴦丸|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (mandarin duck) patterns becoming standardized.
By the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the practice of a11ointing Crown Princes ceased, leading to a temporary discontinuation of the Ōni robe. The custom revived in the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}} with the investiture of Crown Princes like Prince Asahito (later {{ill|Emperor Higashiyama|ja|東山天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft}}). However, records suggest some Crown Princes initially wore reddish-brown robes called "red robes," a term potentially synonymous with Ōni.
In the {{ill|Reiwa era|ja|令和|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the robe is worn by the imperial heir, {{ill|Fumihito, Prince Akishino|ja|秋篠宮文仁親王|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, during ceremonies.<ref>[https://www.sankei.com/article/20201108-GFKCPJRR3RJDHLW3MWBDGY3BAY/ "Ceremonial attire 'rising sun's color' specified in the Nara period"], ''Sankei News'', November 8, 2020.</ref>
== Design ==
Since the time of Prince Asahito, the robe’s crest has featured a flower-shaped frame with a downward-facing {{ill|oshidori|ja|鴛鴦|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (mandarin duck) motif. This design remains unchanged.
According to regulations from the late Edo period, under both the {{ill|Yamashina family|ja|山科家|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Takakura family|ja|高倉家|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, winter robes for mature Crown Princes used yellow silk linings, while younger princes wore matching yellow linings for their {{ill|Kettetsu|ja|闕腋袍|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. The placement of crests on the fabric adhered to a fixed grid without staggered alignment, similar to the Emperor’s robes.
The material and weaving methods evolved in modern times, but the arrangement of patterns became less dense for younger princes. A controversy arose during the investiture of Crown Prince Hirohito (later {{ill|Emperor Shōwa|ja|昭和天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft}}), questioning whether the single mandarin duck should be replaced by a paired design or even by a crane motif. Ultimately, the original pattern was retained based on consultations with Kyoto noble families.

Since the time of Prince Asahito, the robe’s crest has featured a flower-shaped frame with a downward-facing {{ill|oshidori|ja|鴛鴦|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (mandarin duck) motif. This design remains unchanged.
According to regulations from the late Edo period, under both the {{ill|Yamashina family|ja|山科家|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Takakura family|ja|高倉家|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, winter robes for mature Crown Princes used yellow silk linings, while younger princes wore matching yellow linings for their {{ill|Kettetsu|ja|闕腋袍|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. The placement of crests on the fabric adhered to a fixed grid without staggered alignment, similar to the Emperor’s robes.
The material and weaving methods evolved in modern times, but the arrangement of patterns became less dense for younger princes. A controversy arose during the investiture of Crown Prince Hirohito (later {{ill|Emperor Shōwa|ja|昭和天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft}}), questioning whether the single mandarin duck should be replaced by a paired design or even by a crane motif. Ultimately, the original pattern was retained based on consultations with Kyoto noble families.

{{Infobox webcolor|title=Ōni (Crown Prince's Ceremonial Color)|hex=F47A55}} [[File:Prince Naruhito wearing Sokutai.jpg|thumb|200px|On [[November 12, 1990]], Crown Prince [[Naruhito]] wearing the Ōni robe at the [[2019 Japanese imperial transition|Sokutai ceremony]] for Emperor [[Akihito]].]] [[File:Emperor Naruhito and Empress Masako in formal wedding robes.jpg|thumb|300px|In [[1993]], the wedding ceremony of Crown Prince Naruhito and [[Masako Owada]]]] '''Ōni robe''' (黄丹袍, おうにのほう) is the {{ill|sokutai|ja|束帯|qq=draft|qq=draft}} attire worn by the {{ill|Crown Prince of Japan|ja|皇太子|qq=draft|qq=draft}} or {{ill|imperial heir|ja|皇嗣|qq=draft|qq=draft}} during ceremonial occasions. The name is derived from the color {{ill|Ōni|ja|黄丹|1=Ōdan|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, a shade achieved through a dyeing process using {{ill|Gardenia jasminoides|ja|クチナシ|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|safflower|ja|紅花|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
== History ==
The {{ill|Kōrozen|ja|黄櫨染御袍|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, worn by the Emperor, originated in early {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}} as an adaptation of the Chinese ceremonial garment {{ill|Sha Huang|zh|赭黄袍|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, replacing earlier white robes. In contrast, the origins of Ōni in Japan date back to the {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老律令|qq=draft|qq=draft}} during the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, where it was designated in the "Dress Code" for the Crown Prince’s ceremonial and formal robes. It is believed to continue the tradition of the {{ill|Hanezuiro|ja|朱華色|qq=draft|qq=draft}} lineage, unrelated to Chinese customs where early Tang princes wore purple robes.
Ōni symbolizes the rising sun, and its use was strictly reserved for the Crown Prince, designated as a {{ill|forbidden color|ja|禁色|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
During the early Heian period, the Crown Prince’s ceremonial attire transitioned to red robes (九章服), and by the mid-Heian period, its use in court diminished. However, in formal dress (朝服), Ōni persisted, with {{ill|oshidori|ja|鴛鴦丸|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (mandarin duck) patterns becoming standardized.
By the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the practice of a11ointing Crown Princes ceased, leading to a temporary discontinuation of the Ōni robe. The custom revived in the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}} with the investiture of Crown Princes like Prince Asahito (later {{ill|Emperor Higashiyama|ja|東山天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft}}). However, records suggest some Crown Princes initially wore reddish-brown robes called "red robes," a term potentially synonymous with Ōni.
In the {{ill|Reiwa era|ja|令和|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the robe is worn by the imperial heir, {{ill|Fumihito, Prince Akishino|ja|秋篠宮文仁親王|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, during ceremonies.<ref>[https://www.sankei.com/article/20201108-GFKCPJRR3RJDHLW3MWBDGY3BAY/ "Ceremonial attire 'rising sun's color' specified in the Nara period"], ''Sankei News'', November 8, 2020.</ref>
== Design ==
Since the time of Prince Asahito, the robe’s crest has featured a flower-shaped frame with a downward-facing {{ill|oshidori|ja|鴛鴦|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (mandarin duck) motif. This design remains unchanged.
According to regulations from the late Edo period, under both the {{ill|Yamashina family|ja|山科家|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Takakura family|ja|高倉家|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, winter robes for mature Crown Princes used yellow silk linings, while younger princes wore matching yellow linings for their {{ill|Kettetsu|ja|闕腋袍|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. The placement of crests on the fabric adhered to a fixed grid without staggered alignment, similar to the Emperor’s robes.
The material and weaving methods evolved in modern times, but the arrangement of patterns became less dense for younger princes. A controversy arose during the investiture of Crown Prince Hirohito (later {{ill|Emperor Shōwa|ja|昭和天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft}}), questioning whether the single mandarin duck should be replaced by a paired design or even by a crane motif. Ultimately, the original pattern was retained based on consultations with Kyoto noble families.

The custom revived in the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}} with the investiture of Crown Princes like Prince Asahito (later {{ill|Emperor Higashiyama|ja|東山天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft}}). == Design ==
Since the time of Prince Asahito, the robe’s crest has featured a flower-shaped frame with a downward-facing {{ill|oshidori|ja|鴛鴦|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (mandarin duck) motif. This design remains unchanged.

A controversy arose during the investiture of Crown Prince Hirohito (later {{ill|Emperor Shōwa|ja|昭和天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft}}), questioning whether the single mandarin duck should be replaced by a paired design or even by a crane motif. Ultimately, the original pattern was retained based on consultations with Kyoto noble families.

revision.comment: 53 revisions imported: Bot: import full history from ja:黄丹袍 up to rev 90984603
revision.text:
{{Infobox webcolor|title=黄丹（皇太子の束帯の色）|hex=F47A55}} [[File:Prince Naruhito wearing Sokutai.jpg|thumb|200px|[[1990年]]（[[平成]]2年）[[11月12日]]、第125代天皇[[明仁]]の[[即位礼正殿の儀]]で黄丹袍に身を包む皇太子[[徳仁親王]]]] [[File:Emperor Naruhito and Empress Masako in formal wedding robes.jpg|thumb|300px|[[1993年]]（平成5年）、皇太子徳仁親王と[[小和田雅子]]の[[皇太子徳仁親王と小和田雅子の結婚の儀|結婚の儀]]]] '''黄丹袍'''（おうにのほう）とは[[皇太子]]もしくは[[皇嗣]]が[[儀式]]の際に着用する[[束帯]]装束の[[袍]]のことである。この名前は[[クチナシ|梔子]]（くちなし）と[[ベニバナ|紅花]]（べにばな）で染めた[[色名]]の[[黄丹]]からである。
== 沿革 == [[天皇]]が着用した[[黄櫨染御袍]]は[[中国]]の赭黄袍を起源として[[平安時代]]初期に導入された（それ以前の天皇の袍は白色であったと推定されている）のに対し、黄丹色の日本での起源はさらに古く、[[奈良時代]]の『[[養老律令]]』の「衣服令」において皇太子の[[礼服 (宮中)|礼服]]（らいふく）と[[朝服]]の「衣」の色と規定されている。（それ以前の[[朱華色]]（はねずいろ）の系統を引くものと思われる）なお唐代初期には皇太子の常服（日本の朝服の祖形）の規定がなかったが、のちには紫袍を使用したので（『新唐書』車服志）、少なくとも黄丹は唐の模倣ではない。
黄丹の色は昇る旭日を象徴したものでもあり、皇太子以外使用することができない色で[[禁色]]であった。
[[平安時代]]前期には皇太子の礼服は赤色の九章服になったが（さらに[[朝賀]]の廃止された平安中期以降は、皇太子の礼服の着装例がない）、朝服においてはその後も使用され、[[文様]]も鴛鴦丸（えんおうまる）が使われることが一般化した。
[[室町時代]]に至り、[[立太子]]が中絶し、皇位継承者は親王のままで即位することが一般化すると、黄丹袍は中絶した。
[[江戸時代]]に至り、朝仁親王（[[東山天皇]]）の立太子以降皇太子の制度が復活するが、当初は赤色袍と称して、赤茶色の袍をもちいることがあったらしく、裂が各所に現存している。なお皇太子の「赤色袍」は『台記』などにみえるが、黄丹の異名であったものか（黄丹に似たオレンジ色の[[検非違使]]の[[朱紱]]も平安中期以降は「赤衣」とよばれたという事実がある）、別のものであったものか不明である。
[[令和]]時代は皇嗣である[[秋篠宮文仁親王]]が儀式が行われる場合に着用している<ref>[https://www.sankei.com/article/20201108-GFKCPJRR3RJDHLW3MWBDGY3BAY/ 【立皇嗣の礼】装束「昇る朝日の色」奈良時代に規定 - 産経ニュース] 2020年11月8日</ref>。
== 形状 == 朝仁親王以来、[[紋]]は花形の枠に下向きになった[[鴛鴦]]（おしどり）一羽を収めた（字義上は鴛がオスで鴦がメスだが、ここでは雄のみ）もので、定型化して現在まで変わっていない。
[[近世]]後期の規定では、[[衣紋道]]の[[山科家|山科流]]・[[高倉家|高倉流]]ともに冬の場合、元服後の縫腋袍の仕立てのときは裏を黄平絹、[[元服]]前の[[闕腋袍]]の仕立てのときは裏を黄丹平絹の表裏同色とする。紋の配置は升目状に縦横一列に配して、普通の装束地のように一段ごとに紋の位置をずらすことはしない。これは黄櫨染も同じである。
また闕腋のときは、未成年のゆえをもって鴛鴦丸が間を詰めて織られ（総じて装束では、若年ほど紋が小さく密である）る。また冬の生地は、未成年の闕腋であっても浮織にはせずに、三枚綾地に六枚綾で紋をあらわしたいわゆる固地綾で、夏は穀（禾→系）という紗で裏をつけない。
[[近代]]においてもほぼこの制度を継承するが、紋の配置については、闕腋のときも近世ほど間を詰めていない。また裕仁親王（[[昭和天皇]]）立太子に先立ち、「鴛鴦」といいながら一羽であることが問題視され、二羽を組み合わせた丸文及び鴛鴦丸を鶴丸に代える案が生じたが、京都在住華族で、衣紋講習会の講師であった山科言綏らに諮問の結果とりやめになった。
== 関連項目 == * [[麹塵袍]]（きくじんのほう）
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
== 外部リンク == * [http://yuusoku.jp/ 有職文化研究所] * [http://www.iz2.or.jp/ 風俗博物館]
{{デフォルトソート:おうにのほう}} [[Category:宮廷・公家装束]]

[[令和]]時代は皇嗣である[[秋篠宮文仁親王]]が儀式が行われる場合に着用している<ref>[https://www.sankei.com/article/20201108-GFKCPJRR3RJDHLW3MWBDGY3BAY/ 【立皇嗣の礼】装束「昇る朝日の色」奈良時代に規定 - 産経ニュース] 2020年11月8日</ref>。

{{Infobox webcolor|title=Ōni (Crown Prince's Ceremonial Color)|hex=F47A55}} [[File:Prince Naruhito wearing Sokutai.jpg|thumb|200px|On [[November 12, 1990]], Crown Prince [[Naruhito]] wearing the Ōni robe at the [[2019 Japanese imperial transition|Sokutai ceremony]] for Emperor [[Akihito]].]] [[File:Emperor Naruhito and Empress Masako in formal wedding robes.jpg|thumb|300px|In [[1993]], the wedding ceremony of Crown Prince Naruhito and [[Masako Owada]]]] '''Ōni robe''' (黄丹袍, おうにのほう) is the {{ill|sokutai|ja|束帯|qq=draft|qq=draft}} attire worn by the {{ill|Crown Prince of Japan|ja|皇太子|qq=draft|qq=draft}} or {{ill|imperial heir|ja|皇嗣|qq=draft|qq=draft}} during ceremonial occasions. The name is derived from the color {{ill|Ōni|ja|黄丹|1=Ōdan|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, a shade achieved through a dyeing process using {{ill|Gardenia jasminoides|ja|クチナシ|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|safflower|ja|紅花|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
== History ==
The {{ill|Kōrozen|ja|黄櫨染御袍|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, worn by the Emperor, originated in early {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}} as an adaptation of the Chinese ceremonial garment {{ill|Sha Huang|zh|赭黄袍|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, replacing earlier white robes. In contrast, the origins of Ōni in Japan date back to the {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老律令|qq=draft|qq=draft}} during the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, where it was designated in the "Dress Code" for the Crown Prince’s ceremonial and formal robes. It is believed to continue the tradition of the {{ill|Hanezuiro|ja|朱華色|qq=draft|qq=draft}} lineage, unrelated to Chinese customs where early Tang princes wore purple robes.
Ōni symbolizes the rising sun, and its use was strictly reserved for the Crown Prince, designated as a {{ill|forbidden color|ja|禁色|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
During the early Heian period, the Crown Prince’s ceremonial attire transitioned to red robes (九章服), and by the mid-Heian period, its use in court diminished. However, in formal dress (朝服), Ōni persisted, with {{ill|oshidori|ja|鴛鴦丸|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (mandarin duck) patterns becoming standardized.
By the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the practice of a11ointing Crown Princes ceased, leading to a temporary discontinuation of the Ōni robe. The custom revived in the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}} with the investiture of Crown Princes like Prince Asahito (later {{ill|Emperor Higashiyama|ja|東山天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft}}). However, records suggest some Crown Princes initially wore reddish-brown robes called "red robes," a term potentially synonymous with Ōni.
In the {{ill|Reiwa era|ja|令和|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the robe is worn by the imperial heir, {{ill|Fumihito, Prince Akishino|ja|秋篠宮文仁親王|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, during ceremonies.<ref>[https://www.sankei.com/article/20201108-GFKCPJRR3RJDHLW3MWBDGY3BAY/ "Ceremonial attire 'rising sun's color' specified in the Nara period"], ''Sankei News'', November 8, 2020.</ref>
== Design ==
Since the time of Prince Asahito, the robe’s crest has featured a flower-shaped frame with a downward-facing {{ill|oshidori|ja|鴛鴦|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (mandarin duck) motif. This design remains unchanged.
According to regulations from the late Edo period, under both the {{ill|Yamashina family|ja|山科家|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Takakura family|ja|高倉家|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, winter robes for mature Crown Princes used yellow silk linings, while younger princes wore matching yellow linings for their {{ill|Kettetsu|ja|闕腋袍|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. The placement of crests on the fabric adhered to a fixed grid without staggered alignment, similar to the Emperor’s robes.
The material and weaving methods evolved in modern times, but the arrangement of patterns became less dense for younger princes. A controversy arose during the investiture of Crown Prince Hirohito (later {{ill|Emperor Shōwa|ja|昭和天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft}}), questioning whether the single mandarin duck should be replaced by a paired design or even by a crane motif. Ultimately, the original pattern was retained based on consultations with Kyoto noble families.
