{{Infobox webcolor|title=Ōni (Crown Prince's Ceremonial Color)|hex=F47A55}} [[File:Prince Naruhito wearing Sokutai.jpg|thumb|200px|On [[November 12, 1990]], Crown Prince [[Naruhito]] wearing the Ōni robe at the [[2019 Japanese imperial transition|Sokutai ceremony]] for Emperor [[Akihito]].]] [[File:Emperor Naruhito and Empress Masako in formal wedding robes.jpg|thumb|300px|In [[1993]], the wedding ceremony of Crown Prince Naruhito and [[Masako Owada]]]] '''Ōni robe''' (黄丹袍, おうにのほう) is the {{ill|sokutai|ja|束帯|qq=draft|qq=draft}} attire worn by the {{ill|Crown Prince of Japan|ja|皇太子|qq=draft|qq=draft}} or {{ill|imperial heir|ja|皇嗣|qq=draft|qq=draft}} during ceremonial occasions. The name is derived from the color {{ill|Ōni|ja|黄丹|1=Ōdan|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, a shade achieved through a dyeing process using {{ill|Gardenia jasminoides|ja|クチナシ|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|safflower|ja|紅花|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
== History ==
The {{ill|Kōrozen|ja|黄櫨染御袍|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, worn by the Emperor, originated in early {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}} as an adaptation of the Chinese ceremonial garment {{ill|Sha Huang|zh|赭黄袍|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, replacing earlier white robes. In contrast, the origins of Ōni in Japan date back to the {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老律令|qq=draft|qq=draft}} during the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, where it was designated in the "Dress Code" for the Crown Prince’s ceremonial and formal robes. It is believed to continue the tradition of the {{ill|Hanezuiro|ja|朱華色|qq=draft|qq=draft}} lineage, unrelated to Chinese customs where early Tang princes wore purple robes.
Ōni symbolizes the rising sun, and its use was strictly reserved for the Crown Prince, designated as a {{ill|forbidden color|ja|禁色|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
During the early Heian period, the Crown Prince’s ceremonial attire transitioned to red robes (九章服), and by the mid-Heian period, its use in court diminished. However, in formal dress (朝服), Ōni persisted, with {{ill|oshidori|ja|鴛鴦丸|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (mandarin duck) patterns becoming standardized.
By the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the practice of a11ointing Crown Princes ceased, leading to a temporary discontinuation of the Ōni robe. The custom revived in the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}} with the investiture of Crown Princes like Prince Asahito (later {{ill|Emperor Higashiyama|ja|東山天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft}}). However, records suggest some Crown Princes initially wore reddish-brown robes called "red robes," a term potentially synonymous with Ōni.
In the {{ill|Reiwa era|ja|令和|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the robe is worn by the imperial heir, {{ill|Fumihito, Prince Akishino|ja|秋篠宮文仁親王|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, during ceremonies.<ref>[https://www.sankei.com/article/20201108-GFKCPJRR3RJDHLW3MWBDGY3BAY/ "Ceremonial attire 'rising sun's color' specified in the Nara period"], ''Sankei News'', November 8, 2020.</ref>
== Design ==
Since the time of Prince Asahito, the robe’s crest has featured a flower-shaped frame with a downward-facing {{ill|oshidori|ja|鴛鴦|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (mandarin duck) motif. This design remains unchanged.
According to regulations from the late Edo period, under both the {{ill|Yamashina family|ja|山科家|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Takakura family|ja|高倉家|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, winter robes for mature Crown Princes used yellow silk linings, while younger princes wore matching yellow linings for their {{ill|Kettetsu|ja|闕腋袍|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. The placement of crests on the fabric adhered to a fixed grid without staggered alignment, similar to the Emperor’s robes.
The material and weaving methods evolved in modern times, but the arrangement of patterns became less dense for younger princes. A controversy arose during the investiture of Crown Prince Hirohito (later {{ill|Emperor Shōwa|ja|昭和天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft}}), questioning whether the single mandarin duck should be replaced by a paired design or even by a crane motif. Ultimately, the original pattern was retained based on consultations with Kyoto noble families.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Kikujin robe|ja|麹塵袍|qq=draft|qq=draft}}

It is believed to continue the tradition of the {{ill|Hanezuiro|ja|朱華色|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hanezuiro|qq=}} lineage, unrelated to Chinese customs where early Tang princes wore purple robes.
Ōni symbolizes the rising sun, and its use was strictly reserved for the Crown Prince, designated as a {{ill|Forbidden colors (Japan)|lt=Forbidden colors (Japan)|lt=Forbidden color|ja|禁色|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/forbidden color|qq=}}.
During the early Heian period, the Crown Prince’s ceremonial attire transitioned to red robes (九章服), and by the mid-Heian period, its use in court diminished. However, in formal dress (朝服), Ōni persisted, with {{ill|oshidori|ja|鴛鴦丸|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/oshidori|qq=}} (mandarin duck) patterns becoming standardized.
By the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muromachi period|qq=}}, the practice of a11ointing Crown Princes ceased, leading to a temporary discontinuation of the Ōni robe. The custom revived in the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Edo period|qq=}} with the investiture of Crown Princes like Prince Asahito (later {{ill|Emperor Higashiyama|ja|東山天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Higashiyama|qq=}}). However, records suggest some Crown Princes initially wore reddish-brown robes called "red robes," a term potentially synonymous with Ōni.
In the {{ill|Reiwa era|ja|令和|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Reiwa era|qq=}}, the robe is worn by the imperial heir, {{ill|Fumihito, Prince Akishino|ja|秋篠宮文仁親王|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fumihito, Prince Akishino|qq=}}, during ceremonies.<ref>[https://www.sankei.com/article/20201108-GFKCPJRR3RJDHLW3MWBDGY3BAY/ "Ceremonial attire 'rising sun's color' specified in the Nara period"], ''Sankei News'', November 8, 2020.</ref>
== Design ==
Since the time of Prince Asahito, the robe’s crest has featured a flower-shaped frame with a downward-facing {{ill|oshidori|ja|鴛鴦|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/oshidori|qq=}} (mandarin duck) motif. This design remains unchanged.
According to regulations from the late Edo period, under both the {{ill|Yamashina family|ja|山科家|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashina family|qq=}} and {{ill|Takakura family|ja|高倉家|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takakura family|qq=}}, winter robes for mature Crown Princes used yellow silk linings, while younger princes wore matching yellow linings for their {{ill|Kettetsu|ja|闕腋袍|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kettetsu|qq=}}. The placement of crests on the fabric adhered to a fixed grid without staggered alignment, similar to the Emperor’s robes.
The material and weaving methods evolved in modern times, but the arrangement of patterns became less dense for younger princes. A controversy arose during the investiture of Crown Prince Hirohito (later {{ill|Emperor Shōwa|ja|昭和天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Shōwa|qq=}}), questioning whether the single mandarin duck should be replaced by a paired design or even by a crane motif. Ultimately, the original pattern was retained based on consultations with Kyoto noble families.

The name is derived from the color {{ill|Ōni|ja|黄丹|1=Ōdan|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōni|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōdan|qq=}}, a shade achieved through a dyeing process using [[:en:Gardenia jasminoides|Gardenia jasminoides]] and {{ill|safflower|ja|紅花|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/safflower|qq=}}.
== History ==
The {{ill|Kōrozen|ja|黄櫨染御袍|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōrozen|qq=}}, worn by the Emperor, originated in early [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] as an adaptation of the Chinese ceremonial garment {{ill|Sha Huang|zh|赭黄袍|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sha Huang|qq=}}, replacing earlier white robes. In contrast, the origins of Ōni in Japan date back to the [[:en:Yōrō Code|Yōrō Code]] during the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]], where it was designated in the "Dress Code" for the Crown Prince’s ceremonial and formal robes. It is believed to continue the tradition of the {{ill|Hanezuiro|ja|朱華色|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hanezuiro|qq=}} lineage, unrelated to Chinese customs where early Tang princes wore purple robes.
Ōni symbolizes the rising sun, and its use was strictly reserved for the Crown Prince, designated as a [[:en:Forbidden colors (Japan)|Forbidden color]].
During the early Heian period, the Crown Prince’s ceremonial attire transitioned to red robes (九章服), and by the mid-Heian period, its use in court diminished. However, in formal dress (朝服), Ōni persisted, with {{ill|oshidori|ja|鴛鴦丸|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/oshidori|qq=}} (mandarin duck) patterns becoming standardized.
By the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], the practice of a11ointing Crown Princes ceased, leading to a temporary discontinuation of the Ōni robe. The custom revived in the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] with the investiture of Crown Princes like Prince Asahito (later [[:en:Emperor Higashiyama|Emperor Higashiyama]]). However, records suggest some Crown Princes initially wore reddish-brown robes called "red robes," a term potentially synonymous with Ōni.
In the [[:en:Reiwa era|Reiwa era]], the robe is worn by the imperial heir, [[:en:Fumihito, Crown Prince of Japan|Fumihito, Prince Akishino]], during ceremonies.<ref>[https://www.sankei.com/article/20201108-GFKCPJRR3RJDHLW3MWBDGY3BAY/ "Ceremonial attire 'rising sun's color' specified in the Nara period"], ''Sankei News'', November 8, 2020.</ref>

Since the time of Prince Asahito, the robe’s crest has featured a flower-shaped frame with a downward-facing {{ill|oshidori|ja|鴛鴦|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/oshidori|qq=}} (mandarin duck) motif. This design remains unchanged.
According to regulations from the late Edo period, under both the {{ill|Yamashina family|ja|山科家|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashina family|qq=}} and {{ill|Takakura family|ja|高倉家|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takakura family|qq=}}, winter robes for mature Crown Princes used yellow silk linings, while younger princes wore matching yellow linings for their {{ill|Kettetsu|ja|闕腋袍|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kettetsu|qq=}}. The placement of crests on the fabric adhered to a fixed grid without staggered alignment, similar to the Emperor’s robes.
The material and weaving methods evolved in modern times, but the arrangement of patterns became less dense for younger princes. A controversy arose during the investiture of Crown Prince Hirohito (later [[:en:Hirohito|Emperor Shōwa]]), questioning whether the single mandarin duck should be replaced by a paired design or even by a crane motif. Ultimately, the original pattern was retained based on consultations with Kyoto noble families.

The custom revived in the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] with the investiture of Crown Princes like Prince Asahito (later [[:en:Emperor Higashiyama|Emperor Higashiyama]]). In the [[:en:Reiwa era|Reiwa era]], the robe is worn by the imperial heir, [[:en:Fumihito, Crown Prince of Japan|Fumihito, Prince Akishino]], during ceremonies.<ref>[https://www.sankei.com/article/20201108-GFKCPJRR3RJDHLW3MWBDGY3BAY/ "Ceremonial attire 'rising sun's color' specified in the Nara period"], ''Sankei News'', November 8, 2020.</ref>
== Design ==
Since the time of Prince Asahito, the robe’s crest has featured a flower-shaped frame with a downward-facing {{ill|oshidori|ja|鴛鴦|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/oshidori|qq=}} (mandarin duck) motif. This design remains unchanged.
According to regulations from the late Edo period, under both the {{ill|Yamashina family|ja|山科家|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashina family|qq=}} and {{ill|Takakura family|ja|高倉家|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takakura family|qq=}}, winter robes for mature Crown Princes used yellow silk linings, while younger princes wore matching yellow linings for their {{ill|Kettetsu|ja|闕腋袍|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kettetsu|qq=}}. The placement of crests on the fabric adhered to a fixed grid without staggered alignment, similar to the Emperor’s robes.
The material and weaving methods evolved in modern times, but the arrangement of patterns became less dense for younger princes. A controversy arose during the investiture of Crown Prince Hirohito (later [[:en:Hirohito|Emperor Shōwa]]), questioning whether the single mandarin duck should be replaced by a paired design or even by a crane motif. Ultimately, the original pattern was retained based on consultations with Kyoto noble families.

{{Infobox webcolor|title=Ōni (Crown Prince's Ceremonial Color)|hex=F47A55}} [[File:Prince Naruhito wearing Sokutai.jpg|thumb|200px|On [[November 12, 1990]], Crown Prince [[Naruhito]] wearing the Ōni robe at the [[2019 Japanese imperial transition|Sokutai ceremony]] for Emperor [[Akihito]].]] [[File:Emperor Naruhito and Empress Masako in formal wedding robes.jpg|thumb|300px|In [[1993]], the wedding ceremony of Crown Prince Naruhito and [[Masako Owada]]]] '''Ōni robe''' (黄丹袍, おうにのほう) is the [[:en:Sokutai|sokutai]] attire worn by the [[:en:Crown prince|Crown Prince of Japan]] or {{ill|imperial heir|ja|皇嗣|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/imperial heir|qq=}} during ceremonial occasions. The name is derived from the color {{ill|Ōni|ja|黄丹|1=Ōdan|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōni|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōdan|qq=}}, a shade achieved through a dyeing process using [[:en:Gardenia jasminoides|Gardenia jasminoides]] and {{ill|safflower|ja|紅花|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/safflower|qq=}}.
== History ==
The {{ill|Kōrozen|ja|黄櫨染御袍|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōrozen|qq=}}, worn by the Emperor, originated in early [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] as an adaptation of the Chinese ceremonial garment {{ill|Sha Huang|zh|赭黄袍|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sha Huang|qq=}}, replacing earlier white robes. In contrast, the origins of Ōni in Japan date back to the [[:en:Yōrō Code|Yōrō Code]] during the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]], where it was designated in the "Dress Code" for the Crown Prince’s ceremonial and formal robes. It is believed to continue the tradition of the {{ill|Hanezuiro|ja|朱華色|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hanezuiro|qq=}} lineage, unrelated to Chinese customs where early Tang princes wore purple robes.
Ōni symbolizes the rising sun, and its use was strictly reserved for the Crown Prince, designated as a [[:en:Forbidden colors (Japan)|Forbidden color]].
During the early Heian period, the Crown Prince’s ceremonial attire transitioned to red robes (九章服), and by the mid-Heian period, its use in court diminished. However, in formal dress (朝服), Ōni persisted, with {{ill|oshidori|ja|鴛鴦丸|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/oshidori|qq=}} (mandarin duck) patterns becoming standardized.
By the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], the practice of a11ointing Crown Princes ceased, leading to a temporary discontinuation of the Ōni robe. The custom revived in the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] with the investiture of Crown Princes like Prince Asahito (later [[:en:Emperor Higashiyama|Emperor Higashiyama]]). However, records suggest some Crown Princes initially wore reddish-brown robes called "red robes," a term potentially synonymous with Ōni.
In the [[:en:Reiwa era|Reiwa era]], the robe is worn by the imperial heir, [[:en:Fumihito, Crown Prince of Japan|Fumihito, Prince Akishino]], during ceremonies.<ref>[https://www.sankei.com/article/20201108-GFKCPJRR3RJDHLW3MWBDGY3BAY/ "Ceremonial attire 'rising sun's color' specified in the Nara period"], ''Sankei News'', November 8, 2020.</ref>
== Design ==
Since the time of Prince Asahito, the robe’s crest has featured a flower-shaped frame with a downward-facing {{ill|oshidori|ja|鴛鴦|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/oshidori|qq=}} (mandarin duck) motif. This design remains unchanged.
According to regulations from the late Edo period, under both the {{ill|Yamashina family|ja|山科家|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashina family|qq=}} and {{ill|Takakura family|ja|高倉家|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takakura family|qq=}}, winter robes for mature Crown Princes used yellow silk linings, while younger princes wore matching yellow linings for their {{ill|Kettetsu|ja|闕腋袍|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kettetsu|qq=}}. The placement of crests on the fabric adhered to a fixed grid without staggered alignment, similar to the Emperor’s robes.
The material and weaving methods evolved in modern times, but the arrangement of patterns became less dense for younger princes. A controversy arose during the investiture of Crown Prince Hirohito (later [[:en:Hirohito|Emperor Shōwa]]), questioning whether the single mandarin duck should be replaced by a paired design or even by a crane motif. Ultimately, the original pattern was retained based on consultations with Kyoto noble families.

The name is derived from the color {{ill|Ōni|ja|黄丹|1=Ōdan|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōni|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōdan|qq=}}, a shade achieved through a dyeing process using [[:en:Gardenia jasminoides|Gardenia jasminoides]] and {{ill|safflower|ja|紅花|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/safflower|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ベニバナ}}.
== History ==
The {{ill|Kōrozen|ja|黄櫨染御袍|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōrozen|qq=}}, worn by the Emperor, originated in early [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] as an adaptation of the Chinese ceremonial garment {{ill|Sha Huang|zh|赭黄袍|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sha Huang|qq=}}, replacing earlier white robes. In contrast, the origins of Ōni in Japan date back to the [[:en:Yōrō Code|Yōrō Code]] during the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]], where it was designated in the "Dress Code" for the Crown Prince’s ceremonial and formal robes. It is believed to continue the tradition of the {{ill|Hanezuiro|ja|朱華色|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hanezuiro|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 唐棣色}} lineage, unrelated to Chinese customs where early Tang princes wore purple robes.
Ōni symbolizes the rising sun, and its use was strictly reserved for the Crown Prince, designated as a [[:en:Forbidden colors (Japan)|Forbidden color]].
During the early Heian period, the Crown Prince’s ceremonial attire transitioned to red robes (九章服), and by the mid-Heian period, its use in court diminished. However, in formal dress (朝服), Ōni persisted, with {{ill|oshidori|ja|鴛鴦丸|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/oshidori|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (mandarin duck) patterns becoming standardized.
By the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], the practice of a11ointing Crown Princes ceased, leading to a temporary discontinuation of the Ōni robe. The custom revived in the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] with the investiture of Crown Princes like Prince Asahito (later [[:en:Emperor Higashiyama|Emperor Higashiyama]]). However, records suggest some Crown Princes initially wore reddish-brown robes called "red robes," a term potentially synonymous with Ōni.
In the [[:en:Reiwa era|Reiwa era]], the robe is worn by the imperial heir, [[:en:Fumihito, Crown Prince of Japan|Fumihito, Prince Akishino]], during ceremonies.<ref>[https://www.sankei.com/article/20201108-GFKCPJRR3RJDHLW3MWBDGY3BAY/ "Ceremonial attire 'rising sun's color' specified in the Nara period"], ''Sankei News'', November 8, 2020.</ref>
== Design ==
Since the time of Prince Asahito, the robe’s crest has featured a flower-shaped frame with a downward-facing {{ill|oshidori|ja|鴛鴦|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/oshidori|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to オシドリ}} (mandarin duck) motif. This design remains unchanged.
According to regulations from the late Edo period, under both the {{ill|Yamashina family|ja|山科家|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashina family|qq=}} and {{ill|Takakura family|ja|高倉家|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takakura family|qq=}}, winter robes for mature Crown Princes used yellow silk linings, while younger princes wore matching yellow linings for their {{ill|Kettetsu|ja|闕腋袍|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kettetsu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 袍}}. The placement of crests on the fabric adhered to a fixed grid without staggered alignment, similar to the Emperor’s robes.
The material and weaving methods evolved in modern times, but the arrangement of patterns became less dense for younger princes. A controversy arose during the investiture of Crown Prince Hirohito (later [[:en:Hirohito|Emperor Shōwa]]), questioning whether the single mandarin duck should be replaced by a paired design or even by a crane motif. Ultimately, the original pattern was retained based on consultations with Kyoto noble families.

Since the time of Prince Asahito, the robe’s crest has featured a flower-shaped frame with a downward-facing {{ill|oshidori|ja|鴛鴦|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/oshidori|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to オシドリ}} (mandarin duck) motif. This design remains unchanged.
According to regulations from the late Edo period, under both the {{ill|Yamashina family|ja|山科家|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashina family|qq=}} and {{ill|Takakura family|ja|高倉家|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takakura family|qq=}}, winter robes for mature Crown Princes used yellow silk linings, while younger princes wore matching yellow linings for their {{ill|Kettetsu|ja|闕腋袍|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kettetsu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 袍}}. The placement of crests on the fabric adhered to a fixed grid without staggered alignment, similar to the Emperor’s robes.
The material and weaving methods evolved in modern times, but the arrangement of patterns became less dense for younger princes. A controversy arose during the investiture of Crown Prince Hirohito (later [[:en:Hirohito|Emperor Shōwa]]), questioning whether the single mandarin duck should be replaced by a paired design or even by a crane motif. Ultimately, the original pattern was retained based on consultations with Kyoto noble families.

revision.text:
{{Infobox webcolor|title=Ōni (Crown Prince's Ceremonial Color)|hex=F47A55}} [[File:Prince Naruhito wearing Sokutai.jpg|thumb|200px|On [[November 12, 1990]], Crown Prince [[Naruhito]] wearing the Ōni robe at the [[:en:2019 Japanese imperial transition|Sokutai ceremony]] for Emperor [[:en:Akihito|Akihito]].]] [[File:Emperor Naruhito and Empress Masako in formal wedding robes.jpg|thumb|300px|In [[1993]], the wedding ceremony of Crown Prince Naruhito and [[Masako Owada]]]] '''Ōni robe''' (黄丹袍, おうにのほう) is the [[:en:Sokutai|sokutai]] attire worn by the [[:en:Crown prince|Crown Prince of Japan]] or {{ill|imperial heir|ja|皇嗣|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/imperial heir|qq=}} during ceremonial occasions. The name is derived from the color {{ill|Ōni|ja|黄丹|1=Ōdan|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōni|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōdan|qq=}}, a shade achieved through a dyeing process using [[:en:Gardenia jasminoides|Gardenia jasminoides]] and {{ill|safflower|ja|紅花|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/safflower|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ベニバナ}}.
== History ==
The {{ill|Kōrozen|ja|黄櫨染御袍|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōrozen|qq=}}, worn by the Emperor, originated in early [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] as an adaptation of the Chinese ceremonial garment {{ill|Sha Huang|zh|赭黄袍|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sha Huang|qq=}}, replacing earlier white robes. In contrast, the origins of Ōni in Japan date back to the [[:en:Yōrō Code|Yōrō Code]] during the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]], where it was designated in the "Dress Code" for the Crown Prince’s ceremonial and formal robes. It is believed to continue the tradition of the {{ill|Hanezuiro|ja|朱華色|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hanezuiro|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 唐棣色}} lineage, unrelated to Chinese customs where early Tang princes wore purple robes.
Ōni symbolizes the rising sun, and its use was strictly reserved for the Crown Prince, designated as a [[:en:Forbidden colors (Japan)|Forbidden color]].
During the early Heian period, the Crown Prince’s ceremonial attire transitioned to red robes (九章服), and by the mid-Heian period, its use in court diminished. However, in formal dress (朝服), Ōni persisted, with {{ill|oshidori|ja|鴛鴦丸|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/oshidori|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (mandarin duck) patterns becoming standardized.
By the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], the practice of a11ointing Crown Princes ceased, leading to a temporary discontinuation of the Ōni robe. The custom revived in the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] with the investiture of Crown Princes like Prince Asahito (later [[:en:Emperor Higashiyama|Emperor Higashiyama]]). However, records suggest some Crown Princes initially wore reddish-brown robes called "red robes," a term potentially synonymous with Ōni.
In the [[:en:Reiwa era|Reiwa era]], the robe is worn by the imperial heir, [[:en:Fumihito, Crown Prince of Japan|Fumihito, Prince Akishino]], during ceremonies.<ref>[https://www.sankei.com/article/20201108-GFKCPJRR3RJDHLW3MWBDGY3BAY/ "Ceremonial attire 'rising sun's color' specified in the Nara period"], ''Sankei News'', November 8, 2020.</ref>
== Design ==
Since the time of Prince Asahito, the robe’s crest has featured a flower-shaped frame with a downward-facing {{ill|oshidori|ja|鴛鴦|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/oshidori|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to オシドリ}} (mandarin duck) motif. This design remains unchanged.
According to regulations from the late Edo period, under both the {{ill|Yamashina family|ja|山科家|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashina family|qq=}} and {{ill|Takakura family|ja|高倉家|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takakura family|qq=}}, winter robes for mature Crown Princes used yellow silk linings, while younger princes wore matching yellow linings for their {{ill|Kettetsu|ja|闕腋袍|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kettetsu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 袍}}. The placement of crests on the fabric adhered to a fixed grid without staggered alignment, similar to the Emperor’s robes.
The material and weaving methods evolved in modern times, but the arrangement of patterns became less dense for younger princes. A controversy arose during the investiture of Crown Prince Hirohito (later [[:en:Hirohito|Emperor Shōwa]]), questioning whether the single mandarin duck should be replaced by a paired design or even by a crane motif. Ultimately, the original pattern was retained based on consultations with Kyoto noble families.

revision.comment: Text replacement - "{{translated page|" to "[[Category:pages with translation template added]]{{translated page|"
revision.text:
{{Infobox webcolor|title=Ōni (Crown Prince's Ceremonial Color)|hex=F47A55}} [[File:Prince Naruhito wearing Sokutai.jpg|thumb|200px|On [[November 12, 1990]], Crown Prince [[Naruhito]] wearing the Ōni robe at the [[:en:2019 Japanese imperial transition|Sokutai ceremony]] for Emperor [[:en:Akihito|Akihito]].]] [[File:Emperor Naruhito and Empress Masako in formal wedding robes.jpg|thumb|300px|In [[1993]], the wedding ceremony of Crown Prince Naruhito and [[Masako Owada]]]] '''Ōni robe''' (黄丹袍, おうにのほう) is the [[:en:Sokutai|sokutai]] attire worn by the [[:en:Crown prince|Crown Prince of Japan]] or {{ill|imperial heir|ja|皇嗣|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/imperial heir|qq=}} during ceremonial occasions. The name is derived from the color {{ill|Ōni|ja|黄丹|1=Ōdan|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōni|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōdan|qq=}}, a shade achieved through a dyeing process using [[:en:Gardenia jasminoides|Gardenia jasminoides]] and {{ill|safflower|ja|紅花|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/safflower|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ベニバナ}}.
== History ==
The {{ill|Kōrozen|ja|黄櫨染御袍|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōrozen|qq=}}, worn by the Emperor, originated in early [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] as an adaptation of the Chinese ceremonial garment {{ill|Sha Huang|zh|赭黄袍|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sha Huang|qq=}}, replacing earlier white robes. In contrast, the origins of Ōni in Japan date back to the [[:en:Yōrō Code|Yōrō Code]] during the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]], where it was designated in the "Dress Code" for the Crown Prince’s ceremonial and formal robes. It is believed to continue the tradition of the {{ill|Hanezuiro|ja|朱華色|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hanezuiro|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 唐棣色}} lineage, unrelated to Chinese customs where early Tang princes wore purple robes.
Ōni symbolizes the rising sun, and its use was strictly reserved for the Crown Prince, designated as a [[:en:Forbidden colors (Japan)|Forbidden color]].
During the early Heian period, the Crown Prince’s ceremonial attire transitioned to red robes (九章服), and by the mid-Heian period, its use in court diminished. However, in formal dress (朝服), Ōni persisted, with {{ill|oshidori|ja|鴛鴦丸|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/oshidori|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (mandarin duck) patterns becoming standardized.
By the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], the practice of a11ointing Crown Princes ceased, leading to a temporary discontinuation of the Ōni robe. The custom revived in the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] with the investiture of Crown Princes like Prince Asahito (later [[:en:Emperor Higashiyama|Emperor Higashiyama]]). However, records suggest some Crown Princes initially wore reddish-brown robes called "red robes," a term potentially synonymous with Ōni.
In the [[:en:Reiwa era|Reiwa era]], the robe is worn by the imperial heir, [[:en:Fumihito, Crown Prince of Japan|Fumihito, Prince Akishino]], during ceremonies.<ref>[https://www.sankei.com/article/20201108-GFKCPJRR3RJDHLW3MWBDGY3BAY/ "Ceremonial attire 'rising sun's color' specified in the Nara period"], ''Sankei News'', November 8, 2020.</ref>
== Design ==
Since the time of Prince Asahito, the robe’s crest has featured a flower-shaped frame with a downward-facing {{ill|oshidori|ja|鴛鴦|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/oshidori|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to オシドリ}} (mandarin duck) motif. This design remains unchanged.
According to regulations from the late Edo period, under both the {{ill|Yamashina family|ja|山科家|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashina family|qq=}} and {{ill|Takakura family|ja|高倉家|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takakura family|qq=}}, winter robes for mature Crown Princes used yellow silk linings, while younger princes wore matching yellow linings for their {{ill|Kettetsu|ja|闕腋袍|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kettetsu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 袍}}. The placement of crests on the fabric adhered to a fixed grid without staggered alignment, similar to the Emperor’s robes.
The material and weaving methods evolved in modern times, but the arrangement of patterns became less dense for younger princes. A controversy arose during the investiture of Crown Prince Hirohito (later [[:en:Hirohito|Emperor Shōwa]]), questioning whether the single mandarin duck should be replaced by a paired design or even by a crane motif. Ultimately, the original pattern was retained based on consultations with Kyoto noble families.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Kikujin robe|ja|麹塵袍|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kikujin robe|qq=}}
== References ==
{{Reflist}}
== External Links == * [http://yuusoku.jp/ Yūsoku Culture Research Institute] * [http://www.iz2.or.jp/ Costume Museum]

{{Infobox webcolor|title=Ōni (Crown Prince's Ceremonial Color)|hex=F47A55}} [[File:Prince Naruhito wearing Sokutai.jpg|thumb|200px|On [[November 12, 1990]], Crown Prince [[Naruhito]] wearing the Ōni robe at the [[:en:2019 Japanese imperial transition|Sokutai ceremony]] for Emperor [[:en:Akihito|Akihito]].]] [[File:Emperor Naruhito and Empress Masako in formal wedding robes.jpg|thumb|300px|In [[:en:1993|1993]], the wedding ceremony of Crown Prince Naruhito and [[:en:Masako Owada|Masako Owada]]]] '''Ōni robe''' (黄丹袍, おうにのほう) is the [[:en:Sokutai|sokutai]] attire worn by the [[:en:Crown prince|Crown Prince of Japan]] or {{ill|imperial heir|ja|皇嗣|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/imperial heir|qq=}} during ceremonial occasions. The name is derived from the color {{ill|Ōni|ja|黄丹|1=Ōdan|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōni|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōdan|qq=}}, a shade achieved through a dyeing process using [[:en:Gardenia jasminoides|Gardenia jasminoides]] and {{ill|safflower|ja|紅花|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/safflower|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ベニバナ}}.
== History ==
The {{ill|Kōrozen|ja|黄櫨染御袍|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōrozen|qq=}}, worn by the Emperor, originated in early [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] as an adaptation of the Chinese ceremonial garment {{ill|Sha Huang|zh|赭黄袍|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sha Huang|qq=}}, replacing earlier white robes. In contrast, the origins of Ōni in Japan date back to the [[:en:Yōrō Code|Yōrō Code]] during the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]], where it was designated in the "Dress Code" for the Crown Prince’s ceremonial and formal robes. It is believed to continue the tradition of the {{ill|Hanezuiro|ja|朱華色|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hanezuiro|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 唐棣色}} lineage, unrelated to Chinese customs where early Tang princes wore purple robes.
Ōni symbolizes the rising sun, and its use was strictly reserved for the Crown Prince, designated as a [[:en:Forbidden colors (Japan)|Forbidden color]].
During the early Heian period, the Crown Prince’s ceremonial attire transitioned to red robes (九章服), and by the mid-Heian period, its use in court diminished. However, in formal dress (朝服), Ōni persisted, with {{ill|oshidori|ja|鴛鴦丸|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/oshidori|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (mandarin duck) patterns becoming standardized.
By the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], the practice of a11ointing Crown Princes ceased, leading to a temporary discontinuation of the Ōni robe. The custom revived in the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] with the investiture of Crown Princes like Prince Asahito (later [[:en:Emperor Higashiyama|Emperor Higashiyama]]). However, records suggest some Crown Princes initially wore reddish-brown robes called "red robes," a term potentially synonymous with Ōni.
In the [[:en:Reiwa era|Reiwa era]], the robe is worn by the imperial heir, [[:en:Fumihito, Crown Prince of Japan|Fumihito, Prince Akishino]], during ceremonies.<ref>[https://www.sankei.com/article/20201108-GFKCPJRR3RJDHLW3MWBDGY3BAY/ "Ceremonial attire 'rising sun's color' specified in the Nara period"], ''Sankei News'', November 8, 2020.</ref>
== Design ==
Since the time of Prince Asahito, the robe’s crest has featured a flower-shaped frame with a downward-facing {{ill|oshidori|ja|鴛鴦|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/oshidori|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to オシドリ}} (mandarin duck) motif. This design remains unchanged.
According to regulations from the late Edo period, under both the {{ill|Yamashina family|ja|山科家|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashina family|qq=}} and {{ill|Takakura family|ja|高倉家|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takakura family|qq=}}, winter robes for mature Crown Princes used yellow silk linings, while younger princes wore matching yellow linings for their {{ill|Kettetsu|ja|闕腋袍|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kettetsu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 袍}}. The placement of crests on the fabric adhered to a fixed grid without staggered alignment, similar to the Emperor’s robes.
The material and weaving methods evolved in modern times, but the arrangement of patterns became less dense for younger princes. A controversy arose during the investiture of Crown Prince Hirohito (later [[:en:Hirohito|Emperor Shōwa]]), questioning whether the single mandarin duck should be replaced by a paired design or even by a crane motif. Ultimately, the original pattern was retained based on consultations with Kyoto noble families.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Kikujin robe|ja|麹塵袍|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kikujin robe|qq=}}
== References ==
{{Reflist}}
== External Links == * [http://yuusoku.jp/ Yūsoku Culture Research Institute] * [http://www.iz2.or.jp/ Costume Museum]

== History ==
The {{ill|Kōrozen|ja|黄櫨染御袍|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōrozen|qq=}}, worn by the Emperor, originated in early [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] as an adaptation of the Chinese ceremonial garment {{ill|Sha Huang|zh|赭黄袍|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sha Huang|qq=}}, replacing earlier white robes. In contrast, the origins of Ōni in Japan date back to the [[:en:Yōrō Code|Yōrō Code]] during the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]], where it was designated in the "Dress Code" for the Crown Prince’s ceremonial and formal robes. It is believed to continue the tradition of the {{ill|Hanezuiro|ja|朱華色|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hanezuiro|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 唐棣色}} lineage, unrelated to Chinese customs where early Tang princes wore purple robes.
Ōni symbolizes the rising sun, and its use was strictly reserved for the Crown Prince, designated as a [[:en:Forbidden colors (Japan)|Forbidden color]].
During the early Heian period, the Crown Prince’s ceremonial attire transitioned to red robes (九章服), and by the mid-Heian period, its use in court diminished. However, in formal dress (朝服), Ōni persisted, with {{ill|oshidori|ja|鴛鴦丸|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/oshidori|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (mandarin duck) patterns becoming standardized.
By the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], the practice of a11ointing Crown Princes ceased, leading to a temporary discontinuation of the Ōni robe. The custom revived in the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] with the investiture of Crown Princes like Prince Asahito (later [[:en:Emperor Higashiyama|Emperor Higashiyama]]). However, records suggest some Crown Princes initially wore reddish-brown robes called "red robes," a term potentially synonymous with Ōni.
In the [[:en:Reiwa era|Reiwa era]], the robe is worn by the imperial heir, [[:en:Fumihito, Crown Prince of Japan|Fumihito, Prince Akishino]], during ceremonies.<ref>[https://www.sankei.com/article/20201108-GFKCPJRR3RJDHLW3MWBDGY3BAY/ "Ceremonial attire 'rising sun's color' specified in the Nara period"], ''Sankei News'', November 8, 2020.</ref>
== Design ==
Since the time of Prince Asahito, the robe’s crest has featured a flower-shaped frame with a downward-facing {{ill|oshidori|ja|鴛鴦|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/oshidori|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to オシドリ}} (mandarin duck) motif. This design remains unchanged.
According to regulations from the late Edo period, under both the {{ill|Yamashina family|ja|山科家|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashina family|qq=}} and {{ill|Takakura family|ja|高倉家|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takakura family|qq=}}, winter robes for mature Crown Princes used yellow silk linings, while younger princes wore matching yellow linings for their {{ill|Kettetsu|ja|闕腋袍|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kettetsu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 袍}}. The placement of crests on the fabric adhered to a fixed grid without staggered alignment, similar to the Emperor’s robes.
The material and weaving methods evolved in modern times, but the arrangement of patterns became less dense for younger princes. A controversy arose during the investiture of Crown Prince Hirohito (later [[:en:Hirohito|Emperor Shōwa]]), questioning whether the single mandarin duck should be replaced by a paired design or even by a crane motif. Ultimately, the original pattern was retained based on consultations with Kyoto noble families.

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11677718]] Wikidata name: [[Category:Add wikidata]] [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:39, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox webcolor|title=Ōni (Crown Prince's Ceremonial Color)|hex=F47A55}} [[File:Prince Naruhito wearing Sokutai.jpg|thumb|200px|On [[November 12, 1990]], Crown Prince [[:en:Naruhito|Naruhito]] wearing the Ōni robe at the [[:en:2019 Japanese imperial transition|Sokutai ceremony]] for Emperor [[:en:Akihito|Akihito]].]] [[File:Emperor Naruhito and Empress Masako in formal wedding robes.jpg|thumb|300px|In [[:en:1993|1993]], the wedding ceremony of Crown Prince Naruhito and [[:en:Masako Owada|Masako Owada]]]] '''Ōni robe''' (黄丹袍, おうにのほう) is the [[:en:Sokutai|sokutai]] attire worn by the [[:en:Crown prince|Crown Prince of Japan]] or {{ill|imperial heir|ja|皇嗣|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/imperial heir|qq=}} during ceremonial occasions. The name is derived from the color {{ill|Ōni|ja|黄丹|1=Ōdan|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōni|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōdan|qq=}}, a shade achieved through a dyeing process using [[:en:Gardenia jasminoides|Gardenia jasminoides]] and {{ill|safflower|ja|紅花|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/safflower|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ベニバナ}}.

In contrast, the origins of Ōni in Japan date back to the [[:en:Yōrō Code|Yōrō Code]] during the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]], where it was designated in the "Dress Code" for the Crown Prince’s ceremonial and formal robes. It is believed to continue the tradition of the {{ill|Hanezuiro|ja|朱華色|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hanezuiro|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 唐棣色}} lineage, unrelated to Chinese customs where early Tang princes wore purple robes.
Ōni symbolizes the rising sun, and its use was strictly reserved for the Crown Prince, designated as a [[:en:Forbidden colors (Japan)|Forbidden color]].
During the early Heian period, the Crown Prince’s ceremonial attire transitioned to red robes (九章服), and by the mid-Heian period, its use in court diminished. However, in formal dress (朝服), Ōni persisted, with {{ill|oshidori|ja|鴛鴦丸|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/oshidori|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (mandarin duck) patterns becoming standardized.
By the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], the practice of a11ointing Crown Princes ceased, leading to a temporary discontinuation of the Ōni robe. The custom revived in the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] with the investiture of Crown Princes like Prince Asahito (later [[:en:Emperor Higashiyama|Emperor Higashiyama]]). However, records suggest some Crown Princes initially wore reddish-brown robes called "red robes," a term potentially synonymous with Ōni.
In the [[:en:Reiwa era|Reiwa era]], the robe is worn by the imperial heir, [[:en:Fumihito, Crown Prince of Japan|Fumihito, Prince Akishino]], during ceremonies.<ref>[https://www.sankei.com/article/20201108-GFKCPJRR3RJDHLW3MWBDGY3BAY/ "Ceremonial attire 'rising sun's color' specified in the Nara period"], ''Sankei News'', November 8, 2020.</ref>
== Design ==
Since the time of Prince Asahito, the robe’s crest has featured a flower-shaped frame with a downward-facing {{ill|oshidori|ja|鴛鴦|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/oshidori|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to オシドリ|1=Mandarin Duck|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q200339}} (mandarin duck) motif. This design remains unchanged.
According to regulations from the late Edo period, under both the {{ill|Yamashina family|ja|山科家|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashina family|qq=}} and {{ill|Takakura family|ja|高倉家|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takakura family|qq=}}, winter robes for mature Crown Princes used yellow silk linings, while younger princes wore matching yellow linings for their {{ill|Kettetsu|ja|闕腋袍|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kettetsu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 袍|1=hō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q134436881}}. The placement of crests on the fabric adhered to a fixed grid without staggered alignment, similar to the Emperor’s robes.
The material and weaving methods evolved in modern times, but the arrangement of patterns became less dense for younger princes. A controversy arose during the investiture of Crown Prince Hirohito (later [[:en:Hirohito|Emperor Shōwa]]), questioning whether the single mandarin duck should be replaced by a paired design or even by a crane motif. Ultimately, the original pattern was retained based on consultations with Kyoto noble families.

The {{ill|Kōrozen|ja|黄櫨染御袍|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōrozen|qq=}}, worn by the Emperor, originated in early [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] as an adaptation of the Chinese ceremonial garment {{ill|Sha Huang|zh|赭黄袍|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sha Huang|qq=}}, replacing earlier white robes. In contrast, the origins of Ōni in Japan date back to the [[:en:Yōrō Code|Yōrō Code]] during the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]], where it was designated in the "Dress Code" for the Crown Prince’s ceremonial and formal robes. It is believed to continue the tradition of the {{ill|Hanezuiro|ja|朱華色|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hanezuiro|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 唐棣色}} lineage, unrelated to Chinese customs where early Tang princes wore purple robes.
Ōni symbolizes the rising sun, and its use was strictly reserved for the Crown Prince, designated as a [[:en:Forbidden colors (Japan)|Forbidden color]].
During the early Heian period, the Crown Prince’s ceremonial attire transitioned to red robes (九章服), and by the mid-Heian period, its use in court diminished. However, in formal dress (朝服), Ōni persisted, with {{ill|oshidori|ja|鴛鴦丸|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/oshidori|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (mandarin duck) patterns becoming standardized.
By the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], the practice of a11ointing Crown Princes ceased, leading to a temporary discontinuation of the Ōni robe. The custom revived in the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] with the investiture of Crown Princes like Prince Asahito (later [[:en:Emperor Higashiyama|Emperor Higashiyama]]). However, records suggest some Crown Princes initially wore reddish-brown robes called "red robes," a term potentially synonymous with Ōni.
In the [[:en:Reiwa era|Reiwa era]], the robe is worn by the imperial heir, [[:en:Fumihito, Crown Prince of Japan|Fumihito, Prince Akishino]], during ceremonies.<ref>[https://www.sankei.com/article/20201108-GFKCPJRR3RJDHLW3MWBDGY3BAY/ "Ceremonial attire 'rising sun's color' specified in the Nara period"], ''Sankei News'', November 8, 2020.</ref>
== Design ==
Since the time of Prince Asahito, the robe’s crest has featured a flower-shaped frame with a downward-facing {{ill|oshidori|ja|鴛鴦|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/oshidori|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to オシドリ|1=Mandarin Duck|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q200339}} (mandarin duck) motif. This design remains unchanged.
According to regulations from the late Edo period, under both the {{ill|Yamashina family|ja|山科家|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashina family|qq=}} and {{ill|Takakura family|ja|高倉家|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takakura family|qq=}}, winter robes for mature Crown Princes used yellow silk linings, while younger princes wore matching yellow linings for their {{ill|Kettetsu|ja|闕腋袍|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kettetsu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 袍|1=hō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q134436881}}. The placement of crests on the fabric adhered to a fixed grid without staggered alignment, similar to the Emperor’s robes.
The material and weaving methods evolved in modern times, but the arrangement of patterns became less dense for younger princes. A controversy arose during the investiture of Crown Prince Hirohito (later [[:en:Hirohito|Emperor Shōwa]]), questioning whether the single mandarin duck should be replaced by a paired design or even by a crane motif. Ultimately, the original pattern was retained based on consultations with Kyoto noble families.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Kikujin robe|ja|麹塵袍|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kikujin robe|qq=}}

revision.text:
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[d:Q11677718]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:39, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox webcolor|title=Ōni (Crown Prince's Ceremonial Color)|hex=F47A55}} [[File:Prince Naruhito wearing Sokutai.jpg|thumb|200px|On [[November 12, 1990]], Crown Prince [[:en:Naruhito|Naruhito]] wearing the Ōni robe at the [[:en:2019 Japanese imperial transition|Sokutai ceremony]] for Emperor [[:en:Akihito|Akihito]].]] [[File:Emperor Naruhito and Empress Masako in formal wedding robes.jpg|thumb|300px|In [[:en:1993|1993]], the wedding ceremony of Crown Prince Naruhito and [[:en:Masako Owada|Masako Owada]]]] '''Ōni robe''' (黄丹袍, おうにのほう) is the [[:en:Sokutai|sokutai]] attire worn by the [[:en:Crown prince|Crown Prince of Japan]] or {{ill|imperial heir|ja|皇嗣|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/imperial heir|qq=}} during ceremonial occasions. The name is derived from the color {{ill|Ōni|ja|黄丹|1=Ōdan|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōni|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōdan|qq=}}, a shade achieved through a dyeing process using [[:en:Gardenia jasminoides|Gardenia jasminoides]] and {{ill|safflower|ja|紅花|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/safflower|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ベニバナ|1=Carthamus tinctorius|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q156625}}.
== History ==
The {{ill|Kōrozen|ja|黄櫨染御袍|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōrozen|qq=}}, worn by the Emperor, originated in early [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] as an adaptation of the Chinese ceremonial garment {{ill|Sha Huang|zh|赭黄袍|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sha Huang|qq=}}, replacing earlier white robes. In contrast, the origins of Ōni in Japan date back to the [[:en:Yōrō Code|Yōrō Code]] during the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]], where it was designated in the "Dress Code" for the Crown Prince’s ceremonial and formal robes. It is believed to continue the tradition of the {{ill|Hanezuiro|ja|朱華色|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hanezuiro|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 唐棣色}} lineage, unrelated to Chinese customs where early Tang princes wore purple robes.
Ōni symbolizes the rising sun, and its use was strictly reserved for the Crown Prince, designated as a [[:en:Forbidden colors (Japan)|Forbidden color]].
During the early Heian period, the Crown Prince’s ceremonial attire transitioned to red robes (九章服), and by the mid-Heian period, its use in court diminished. However, in formal dress (朝服), Ōni persisted, with {{ill|oshidori|ja|鴛鴦丸|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/oshidori|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (mandarin duck) patterns becoming standardized.
By the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], the practice of a11ointing Crown Princes ceased, leading to a temporary discontinuation of the Ōni robe. The custom revived in the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] with the investiture of Crown Princes like Prince Asahito (later [[:en:Emperor Higashiyama|Emperor Higashiyama]]). However, records suggest some Crown Princes initially wore reddish-brown robes called "red robes," a term potentially synonymous with Ōni.
In the [[:en:Reiwa era|Reiwa era]], the robe is worn by the imperial heir, [[:en:Fumihito, Crown Prince of Japan|Fumihito, Prince Akishino]], during ceremonies.<ref>[https://www.sankei.com/article/20201108-GFKCPJRR3RJDHLW3MWBDGY3BAY/ "Ceremonial attire 'rising sun's color' specified in the Nara period"], ''Sankei News'', November 8, 2020.</ref>
== Design ==
Since the time of Prince Asahito, the robe’s crest has featured a flower-shaped frame with a downward-facing {{ill|oshidori|ja|鴛鴦|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/oshidori|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to オシドリ|1=Mandarin Duck|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q200339}} (mandarin duck) motif. This design remains unchanged.
According to regulations from the late Edo period, under both the {{ill|Yamashina family|ja|山科家|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashina family|qq=}} and {{ill|Takakura family|ja|高倉家|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takakura family|qq=}}, winter robes for mature Crown Princes used yellow silk linings, while younger princes wore matching yellow linings for their {{ill|Kettetsu|ja|闕腋袍|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kettetsu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 袍|1=hō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q134436881}}. The placement of crests on the fabric adhered to a fixed grid without staggered alignment, similar to the Emperor’s robes.
The material and weaving methods evolved in modern times, but the arrangement of patterns became less dense for younger princes. A controversy arose during the investiture of Crown Prince Hirohito (later [[:en:Hirohito|Emperor Shōwa]]), questioning whether the single mandarin duck should be replaced by a paired design or even by a crane motif. Ultimately, the original pattern was retained based on consultations with Kyoto noble families.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Kikujin robe|ja|麹塵袍|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kikujin robe|qq=}}
== References ==
{{Reflist}}
== External Links == * [http://yuusoku.jp/ Yūsoku Culture Research Institute] * [http://www.iz2.or.jp/ Costume Museum]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:黄丹袍]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[d:Q11677718]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:39, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox webcolor|title=Ōni (Crown Prince's Ceremonial Color)|hex=F47A55}} [[File:Prince Naruhito wearing Sokutai.jpg|thumb|200px|On [[November 12, 1990]], Crown Prince [[:en:Naruhito|Naruhito]] wearing the Ōni robe at the [[:en:2019 Japanese imperial transition|Sokutai ceremony]] for Emperor [[:en:Akihito|Akihito]].]] [[File:Emperor Naruhito and Empress Masako in formal wedding robes.jpg|thumb|300px|In [[:en:1993|1993]], the wedding ceremony of Crown Prince Naruhito and [[:en:Masako Owada|Masako Owada]]]] '''Ōni robe''' (黄丹袍, おうにのほう) is the [[:en:Sokutai|sokutai]] attire worn by the [[:en:Crown prince|Crown Prince of Japan]] or {{ill|imperial heir|ja|皇嗣|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/imperial heir|qq=}} during ceremonial occasions. The name is derived from the color {{ill|Ōni|ja|黄丹|1=Ōdan|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōni|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōdan|qq=}}, a shade achieved through a dyeing process using [[:en:Gardenia jasminoides|Gardenia jasminoides]] and {{ill|safflower|ja|紅花|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/safflower|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ベニバナ|1=Carthamus tinctorius|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q156625}}.
== History ==
The {{ill|Kōrozen|ja|黄櫨染御袍|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōrozen|qq=}}, worn by the Emperor, originated in early [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] as an adaptation of the Chinese ceremonial garment {{ill|Sha Huang|zh|赭黄袍|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sha Huang|qq=}}, replacing earlier white robes. In contrast, the origins of Ōni in Japan date back to the [[:en:Yōrō Code|Yōrō Code]] during the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]], where it was designated in the "Dress Code" for the Crown Prince’s ceremonial and formal robes. It is believed to continue the tradition of the {{ill|Hanezuiro|ja|朱華色|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hanezuiro|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 唐棣色}} lineage, unrelated to Chinese customs where early Tang princes wore purple robes.
Ōni symbolizes the rising sun, and its use was strictly reserved for the Crown Prince, designated as a [[:en:Forbidden colors (Japan)|Forbidden color]].
During the early Heian period, the Crown Prince’s ceremonial attire transitioned to red robes (九章服), and by the mid-Heian period, its use in court diminished. However, in formal dress (朝服), Ōni persisted, with {{ill|oshidori|ja|鴛鴦丸|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/oshidori|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (mandarin duck) patterns becoming standardized.
By the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], the practice of a11ointing Crown Princes ceased, leading to a temporary discontinuation of the Ōni robe. The custom revived in the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] with the investiture of Crown Princes like Prince Asahito (later [[:en:Emperor Higashiyama|Emperor Higashiyama]]). However, records suggest some Crown Princes initially wore reddish-brown robes called "red robes," a term potentially synonymous with Ōni.
In the [[:en:Reiwa era|Reiwa era]], the robe is worn by the imperial heir, [[:en:Fumihito, Crown Prince of Japan|Fumihito, Prince Akishino]], during ceremonies.<ref>[https://www.sankei.com/article/20201108-GFKCPJRR3RJDHLW3MWBDGY3BAY/ "Ceremonial attire 'rising sun's color' specified in the Nara period"], ''Sankei News'', November 8, 2020.</ref>
== Design ==
Since the time of Prince Asahito, the robe’s crest has featured a flower-shaped frame with a downward-facing {{ill|oshidori|ja|鴛鴦|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/oshidori|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to オシドリ|1=Mandarin Duck|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q200339}} (mandarin duck) motif. This design remains unchanged.
According to regulations from the late Edo period, under both the {{ill|Yamashina family|ja|山科家|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashina family|qq=}} and {{ill|Takakura family|ja|高倉家|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takakura family|qq=}}, winter robes for mature Crown Princes used yellow silk linings, while younger princes wore matching yellow linings for their {{ill|Kettetsu|ja|闕腋袍|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kettetsu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 袍|1=hō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q134436881}}. The placement of crests on the fabric adhered to a fixed grid without staggered alignment, similar to the Emperor’s robes.
The material and weaving methods evolved in modern times, but the arrangement of patterns became less dense for younger princes. A controversy arose during the investiture of Crown Prince Hirohito (later [[:en:Hirohito|Emperor Shōwa]]), questioning whether the single mandarin duck should be replaced by a paired design or even by a crane motif. Ultimately, the original pattern was retained based on consultations with Kyoto noble families.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Kikujin robe|ja|麹塵袍|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kikujin robe|qq=}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:黄丹袍]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[d:Q11677718]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:39, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox webcolor|title=Ōni (Crown Prince's Ceremonial Color)|hex=F47A55}} [[File:Prince Naruhito wearing Sokutai.jpg|thumb|200px|On [[November 12, 1990]], Crown Prince [[:en:Naruhito|Naruhito]] wearing the Ōni robe at the [[:en:2019 Japanese imperial transition|Sokutai ceremony]] for Emperor [[:en:Akihito|Akihito]].]] [[File:Emperor Naruhito and Empress Masako in formal wedding robes.jpg|thumb|300px|In [[:en:1993|1993]], the wedding ceremony of Crown Prince Naruhito and [[:en:Masako Owada|Masako Owada]]]] '''Ōni robe''' (黄丹袍, おうにのほう) is the [[:en:Sokutai|sokutai]] attire worn by the [[:en:Crown prince|Crown Prince of Japan]] or {{ill|imperial heir|ja|皇嗣|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/imperial heir|qq=}} during ceremonial occasions. The name is derived from the color {{ill|Ōni|ja|黄丹|1=Ōdan|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōni|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōdan|qq=}}, a shade achieved through a dyeing process using [[:en:Gardenia jasminoides|Gardenia jasminoides]] and {{ill|safflower|ja|紅花|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/safflower|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ベニバナ|1=Carthamus tinctorius|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q156625}}.
== History ==
The {{ill|Kōrozen|ja|黄櫨染御袍|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōrozen|qq=}}, worn by the Emperor, originated in early [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] as an adaptation of the Chinese ceremonial garment {{ill|Sha Huang|zh|赭黄袍|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sha Huang|qq=}}, replacing earlier white robes. In contrast, the origins of Ōni in Japan date back to the [[:en:Yōrō Code|Yōrō Code]] during the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]], where it was designated in the "Dress Code" for the Crown Prince’s ceremonial and formal robes. It is believed to continue the tradition of the {{ill|Hanezuiro|ja|朱華色|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hanezuiro|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 唐棣色}} lineage, unrelated to Chinese customs where early Tang princes wore purple robes.
Ōni symbolizes the rising sun, and its use was strictly reserved for the Crown Prince, designated as a [[:en:Forbidden colors (Japan)|Forbidden color]].
During the early Heian period, the Crown Prince’s ceremonial attire transitioned to red robes (九章服), and by the mid-Heian period, its use in court diminished. However, in formal dress (朝服), Ōni persisted, with {{ill|oshidori|ja|鴛鴦丸|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/oshidori|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (mandarin duck) patterns becoming standardized.
By the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], the practice of a11ointing Crown Princes ceased, leading to a temporary discontinuation of the Ōni robe. The custom revived in the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] with the investiture of Crown Princes like Prince Asahito (later [[:en:Emperor Higashiyama|Emperor Higashiyama]]). However, records suggest some Crown Princes initially wore reddish-brown robes called "red robes," a term potentially synonymous with Ōni.
In the [[:en:Reiwa era|Reiwa era]], the robe is worn by the imperial heir, [[:en:Fumihito, Crown Prince of Japan|Fumihito, Prince Akishino]], during ceremonies.<ref>[https://www.sankei.com/article/20201108-GFKCPJRR3RJDHLW3MWBDGY3BAY/ "Ceremonial attire 'rising sun's color' specified in the Nara period"], ''Sankei News'', November 8, 2020.</ref>
== Design ==
Since the time of Prince Asahito, the robe’s crest has featured a flower-shaped frame with a downward-facing {{ill|oshidori|ja|鴛鴦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/oshidori|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to オシドリ|1=Mandarin Duck|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q200339}} (mandarin duck) motif. This design remains unchanged.
According to regulations from the late Edo period, under both the {{ill|Yamashina family|ja|山科家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashina family|qq=}} and {{ill|Takakura family|ja|高倉家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takakura family|qq=}}, winter robes for mature Crown Princes used yellow silk linings, while younger princes wore matching yellow linings for their {{ill|Kettetsu|ja|闕腋袍|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kettetsu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 袍|1=hō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q134436881}}. The placement of crests on the fabric adhered to a fixed grid without staggered alignment, similar to the Emperor’s robes.
The material and weaving methods evolved in modern times, but the arrangement of patterns became less dense for younger princes. A controversy arose during the investiture of Crown Prince Hirohito (later [[:en:Hirohito|Emperor Shōwa]]), questioning whether the single mandarin duck should be replaced by a paired design or even by a crane motif. Ultimately, the original pattern was retained based on consultations with Kyoto noble families.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Kikujin robe|ja|麹塵袍|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kikujin robe|qq=}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:黄丹袍]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[d:Q11677718]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:39, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox webcolor|title=Ōni (Crown Prince's Ceremonial Color)|hex=F47A55}} [[File:Prince Naruhito wearing Sokutai.jpg|thumb|200px|On [[November 12, 1990]], Crown Prince [[:en:Naruhito|Naruhito]] wearing the Ōni robe at the [[:en:2019 Japanese imperial transition|Sokutai ceremony]] for Emperor [[:en:Akihito|Akihito]].]] [[File:Emperor Naruhito and Empress Masako in formal wedding robes.jpg|thumb|300px|In [[:en:1993|1993]], the wedding ceremony of Crown Prince Naruhito and [[:en:Masako Owada|Masako Owada]]]] '''Ōni robe''' (黄丹袍, おうにのほう) is the [[:en:Sokutai|sokutai]] attire worn by the [[:en:Crown prince|Crown Prince of Japan]] or {{ill|imperial heir|ja|皇嗣|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/imperial heir|qq=}} during ceremonial occasions. The name is derived from the color {{ill|Ōni|ja|黄丹|1=Ōdan|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōni|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōdan|qq=}}, a shade achieved through a dyeing process using [[:en:Gardenia jasminoides|Gardenia jasminoides]] and {{ill|safflower|ja|紅花|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/safflower|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ベニバナ|1=Carthamus tinctorius|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q156625}}.
== History ==
The {{ill|Kōrozen|ja|黄櫨染御袍|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōrozen|qq=}}, worn by the Emperor, originated in early [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] as an adaptation of the Chinese ceremonial garment {{ill|Sha Huang|zh|赭黄袍|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sha Huang|qq=}}, replacing earlier white robes. In contrast, the origins of Ōni in Japan date back to the [[:en:Yōrō Code|Yōrō Code]] during the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]], where it was designated in the "Dress Code" for the Crown Prince’s ceremonial and formal robes. It is believed to continue the tradition of the {{ill|Hanezuiro|ja|朱華色|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hanezuiro|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 唐棣色}} lineage, unrelated to Chinese customs where early Tang princes wore purple robes.
Ōni symbolizes the rising sun, and its use was strictly reserved for the Crown Prince, designated as a [[:en:Forbidden colors (Japan)|Forbidden color]].
During the early Heian period, the Crown Prince’s ceremonial attire transitioned to red robes (九章服), and by the mid-Heian period, its use in court diminished. However, in formal dress (朝服), Ōni persisted, with {{ill|oshidori|ja|鴛鴦丸|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/oshidori|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (mandarin duck) patterns becoming standardized.
By the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], the practice of a11ointing Crown Princes ceased, leading to a temporary discontinuation of the Ōni robe. The custom revived in the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] with the investiture of Crown Princes like Prince Asahito (later [[:en:Emperor Higashiyama|Emperor Higashiyama]]). However, records suggest some Crown Princes initially wore reddish-brown robes called "red robes," a term potentially synonymous with Ōni.
In the [[:en:Reiwa era|Reiwa era]], the robe is worn by the imperial heir, [[:en:Fumihito, Crown Prince of Japan|Fumihito, Prince Akishino]], during ceremonies.<ref>[https://www.sankei.com/article/20201108-GFKCPJRR3RJDHLW3MWBDGY3BAY/ "Ceremonial attire 'rising sun's color' specified in the Nara period"], ''Sankei News'', November 8, 2020.</ref>
== Design ==
Since the time of Prince Asahito, the robe’s crest has featured a flower-shaped frame with a downward-facing {{ill|oshidori|ja|鴛鴦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/oshidori|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to オシドリ|1=Mandarin Duck|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q200339}} (mandarin duck) motif. This design remains unchanged.
According to regulations from the late Edo period, under both the {{ill|Yamashina family|ja|山科家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashina family|qq=}} and {{ill|Takakura family|ja|高倉家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takakura family|qq=}}, winter robes for mature Crown Princes used yellow silk linings, while younger princes wore matching yellow linings for their {{ill|Kettetsu|ja|闕腋袍|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kettetsu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 袍|1=hō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q134436881}}. The placement of crests on the fabric adhered to a fixed grid without staggered alignment, similar to the Emperor’s robes.
The material and weaving methods evolved in modern times, but the arrangement of patterns became less dense for younger princes. A controversy arose during the investiture of Crown Prince Hirohito (later [[:en:Hirohito|Emperor Shōwa]]), questioning whether the single mandarin duck should be replaced by a paired design or even by a crane motif. Ultimately, the original pattern was retained based on consultations with Kyoto noble families.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Kikujin robe|ja|麹塵袍|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kikujin robe|qq=}}
== References ==
{{Reflist}}
== External Links == * [http://yuusoku.jp/ Yūsoku Culture Research Institute] * [http://www.iz2.or.jp/ Costume Museum]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:黄丹袍]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:黄丹袍]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:黄丹袍]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[d:Q11677718]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:39, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox webcolor|title=Ōni (Crown Prince's Ceremonial Color)|hex=F47A55}} [[File:Prince Naruhito wearing Sokutai.jpg|thumb|200px|On [[November 12, 1990]], Crown Prince [[:en:Naruhito|Naruhito]] wearing the Ōni robe at the [[:en:2019 Japanese imperial transition|Sokutai ceremony]] for Emperor [[:en:Akihito|Akihito]].]] [[File:Emperor Naruhito and Empress Masako in formal wedding robes.jpg|thumb|300px|In [[:en:1993|1993]], the wedding ceremony of Crown Prince Naruhito and [[:en:Masako Owada|Masako Owada]]]] '''Ōni robe''' (黄丹袍, おうにのほう) is the [[:en:Sokutai|sokutai]] attire worn by the [[:en:Crown prince|Crown Prince of Japan]] or {{ill|imperial heir|ja|皇嗣|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/imperial heir|qq=}} during ceremonial occasions. The name is derived from the color {{ill|Ōni|ja|黄丹|1=Ōdan|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōni|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōdan|qq=}}, a shade achieved through a dyeing process using [[:en:Gardenia jasminoides|Gardenia jasminoides]] and {{ill|safflower|ja|紅花|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/safflower|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ベニバナ|1=Carthamus tinctorius|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q156625}}.
== History ==
The {{ill|Kōrozen|ja|黄櫨染御袍|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōrozen|qq=}}, worn by the Emperor, originated in early [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] as an adaptation of the Chinese ceremonial garment {{ill|Sha Huang|zh|赭黄袍|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sha Huang|qq=}}, replacing earlier white robes. In contrast, the origins of Ōni in Japan date back to the [[:en:Yōrō Code|Yōrō Code]] during the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]], where it was designated in the "Dress Code" for the Crown Prince’s ceremonial and formal robes. It is believed to continue the tradition of the {{ill|Hanezuiro|ja|朱華色|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hanezuiro|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 唐棣色}} lineage, unrelated to Chinese customs where early Tang princes wore purple robes.
Ōni symbolizes the rising sun, and its use was strictly reserved for the Crown Prince, designated as a [[:en:Forbidden colors (Japan)|Forbidden color]].
During the early Heian period, the Crown Prince’s ceremonial attire transitioned to red robes (九章服), and by the mid-Heian period, its use in court diminished. However, in formal dress (朝服), Ōni persisted, with {{ill|oshidori|ja|鴛鴦丸|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/oshidori|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (mandarin duck) patterns becoming standardized.
By the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], the practice of a11ointing Crown Princes ceased, leading to a temporary discontinuation of the Ōni robe. The custom revived in the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] with the investiture of Crown Princes like Prince Asahito (later [[:en:Emperor Higashiyama|Emperor Higashiyama]]). However, records suggest some Crown Princes initially wore reddish-brown robes called "red robes," a term potentially synonymous with Ōni.
In the [[:en:Reiwa era|Reiwa era]], the robe is worn by the imperial heir, [[:en:Fumihito, Crown Prince of Japan|Fumihito, Prince Akishino]], during ceremonies.<ref>[https://www.sankei.com/article/20201108-GFKCPJRR3RJDHLW3MWBDGY3BAY/ "Ceremonial attire 'rising sun's color' specified in the Nara period"], ''Sankei News'', November 8, 2020.</ref>
== Design ==
Since the time of Prince Asahito, the robe’s crest has featured a flower-shaped frame with a downward-facing {{ill|oshidori|ja|鴛鴦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/oshidori|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to オシドリ|1=Mandarin Duck|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q200339}} (mandarin duck) motif. This design remains unchanged.
According to regulations from the late Edo period, under both the {{ill|Yamashina family|ja|山科家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashina family|qq=}} and {{ill|Takakura family|ja|高倉家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takakura family|qq=}}, winter robes for mature Crown Princes used yellow silk linings, while younger princes wore matching yellow linings for their {{ill|Kettetsu|ja|闕腋袍|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kettetsu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 袍|1=hō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q134436881}}. The placement of crests on the fabric adhered to a fixed grid without staggered alignment, similar to the Emperor’s robes.
The material and weaving methods evolved in modern times, but the arrangement of patterns became less dense for younger princes. A controversy arose during the investiture of Crown Prince Hirohito (later [[:en:Hirohito|Emperor Shōwa]]), questioning whether the single mandarin duck should be replaced by a paired design or even by a crane motif. Ultimately, the original pattern was retained based on consultations with Kyoto noble families.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Kikujin robe|ja|麹塵袍|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kikujin robe|qq=}}
== References ==
{{Reflist}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:黄丹袍]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:黄丹袍]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:黄丹袍]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11677718]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:39, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox webcolor|title=Ōni (Crown Prince's Ceremonial Color)|hex=F47A55}} [[File:Prince Naruhito wearing Sokutai.jpg|thumb|200px|On [[November 12, 1990]], Crown Prince [[:en:Naruhito|Naruhito]] wearing the Ōni robe at the [[:en:2019 Japanese imperial transition|Sokutai ceremony]] for Emperor [[:en:Akihito|Akihito]].]] [[File:Emperor Naruhito and Empress Masako in formal wedding robes.jpg|thumb|300px|In [[:en:1993|1993]], the wedding ceremony of Crown Prince Naruhito and [[:en:Masako Owada|Masako Owada]]]] '''Ōni robe''' (黄丹袍, おうにのほう) is the [[:en:Sokutai|sokutai]] attire worn by the [[:en:Crown prince|Crown Prince of Japan]] or {{ill|imperial heir|ja|皇嗣|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/imperial heir|qq=}} during ceremonial occasions. The name is derived from the color {{ill|Ōni|ja|黄丹|1=Ōdan|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōni|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōdan|qq=}}, a shade achieved through a dyeing process using [[:en:Gardenia jasminoides|Gardenia jasminoides]] and {{ill|safflower|ja|紅花|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/safflower|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ベニバナ|1=Carthamus tinctorius|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q156625}}.
== History ==
The {{ill|Kōrozen|ja|黄櫨染御袍|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōrozen|qq=}}, worn by the Emperor, originated in early [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] as an adaptation of the Chinese ceremonial garment {{ill|Sha Huang|zh|赭黄袍|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sha Huang|qq=}}, replacing earlier white robes. In contrast, the origins of Ōni in Japan date back to the [[:en:Yōrō Code|Yōrō Code]] during the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]], where it was designated in the "Dress Code" for the Crown Prince’s ceremonial and formal robes. It is believed to continue the tradition of the {{ill|Hanezuiro|ja|朱華色|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hanezuiro|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 唐棣色}} lineage, unrelated to Chinese customs where early Tang princes wore purple robes.
Ōni symbolizes the rising sun, and its use was strictly reserved for the Crown Prince, designated as a [[:en:Forbidden colors (Japan)|Forbidden color]].
During the early Heian period, the Crown Prince’s ceremonial attire transitioned to red robes (九章服), and by the mid-Heian period, its use in court diminished. However, in formal dress (朝服), Ōni persisted, with {{ill|oshidori|ja|鴛鴦丸|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/oshidori|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (mandarin duck) patterns becoming standardized.
By the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], the practice of a11ointing Crown Princes ceased, leading to a temporary discontinuation of the Ōni robe. The custom revived in the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] with the investiture of Crown Princes like Prince Asahito (later [[:en:Emperor Higashiyama|Emperor Higashiyama]]). However, records suggest some Crown Princes initially wore reddish-brown robes called "red robes," a term potentially synonymous with Ōni.
In the [[:en:Reiwa era|Reiwa era]], the robe is worn by the imperial heir, [[:en:Fumihito, Crown Prince of Japan|Fumihito, Prince Akishino]], during ceremonies.<ref>[https://www.sankei.com/article/20201108-GFKCPJRR3RJDHLW3MWBDGY3BAY/ "Ceremonial attire 'rising sun's color' specified in the Nara period"], ''Sankei News'', November 8, 2020.</ref>
== Design ==
Since the time of Prince Asahito, the robe’s crest has featured a flower-shaped frame with a downward-facing {{ill|oshidori|ja|鴛鴦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/oshidori|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to オシドリ|1=Mandarin Duck|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q200339}} (mandarin duck) motif. This design remains unchanged.
According to regulations from the late Edo period, under both the {{ill|Yamashina family|ja|山科家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashina family|qq=}} and {{ill|Takakura family|ja|高倉家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takakura family|qq=}}, winter robes for mature Crown Princes used yellow silk linings, while younger princes wore matching yellow linings for their {{ill|Kettetsu|ja|闕腋袍|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kettetsu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 袍|1=hō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q134436881}}. The placement of crests on the fabric adhered to a fixed grid without staggered alignment, similar to the Emperor’s robes.
The material and weaving methods evolved in modern times, but the arrangement of patterns became less dense for younger princes. A controversy arose during the investiture of Crown Prince Hirohito (later [[:en:Hirohito|Emperor Shōwa]]), questioning whether the single mandarin duck should be replaced by a paired design or even by a crane motif. Ultimately, the original pattern was retained based on consultations with Kyoto noble families.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Kikujin robe|ja|麹塵袍|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kikujin robe|qq=}}
== References ==
{{Reflist}}
