<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:黄丹袍]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:黄丹袍]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:黄丹袍]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11677718]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:39, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox webcolor|title=Ōni (Crown Prince's Ceremonial Color)|hex=F47A55}} [[File:Prince Naruhito wearing Sokutai.jpg|thumb|200px|On [[November 12, 1990]], Crown Prince {{ill|Naruhito|en|Naruhito|WD=Q217096|lt=Naruhito}} wearing the Ōni robe at the {{ill|2019 Japanese imperial transition|en|2019 Japanese imperial transition|WD=Q44023338|lt=Sokutai ceremony}} for Emperor {{ill|Akihito|en|Akihito|WD=Q37979|lt=Akihito}}.]] [[File:Emperor Naruhito and Empress Masako in formal wedding robes.jpg|thumb|300px|In {{ill|1993|en|1993|WD=Q2065|lt=1993}}, the wedding ceremony of Crown Prince Naruhito and [[:en:Masako Owada|Masako Owada]]]] '''Ōni robe''' (黄丹袍, おうにのほう) is the {{ill|Sokutai|en|Sokutai|WD=Q1156828|lt=sokutai}} attire worn by the {{ill|Crown prince|en|Crown prince|WD=Q207293|lt=Crown Prince of Japan}} or {{ill|imperial heir|ja|皇嗣|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/imperial heir|qq=}} during ceremonial occasions. The name is derived from the color {{ill|Ōni|ja|黄丹|1=Ōdan|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōni|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōdan|qq=}}, a shade achieved through a dyeing process using {{ill|Gardenia jasminoides|en|Gardenia jasminoides|WD=Q1072101|lt=Gardenia jasminoides}} and {{ill|safflower|ja|紅花|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/safflower|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ベニバナ|1=Carthamus tinctorius|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q156625}}.
== History ==
The {{ill|Kōrozen|ja|黄櫨染御袍|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōrozen|qq=}}, worn by the Emperor, originated in early {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}} as an adaptation of the Chinese ceremonial garment {{ill|Sha Huang|zh|赭黄袍|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sha Huang|qq=}}, replacing earlier white robes. In contrast, the origins of Ōni in Japan date back to the {{ill|Yōrō Code|en|Yōrō Code|WD=Q2575743|lt=Yōrō Code}} during the {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|WD=Q189178|lt=Nara period}}, where it was designated in the "Dress Code" for the Crown Prince’s ceremonial and formal robes. It is believed to continue the tradition of the {{ill|Hanezuiro|ja|朱華色|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hanezuiro|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 唐棣色}} lineage, unrelated to Chinese customs where early Tang princes wore purple robes.
Ōni symbolizes the rising sun, and its use was strictly reserved for the Crown Prince, designated as a {{ill|Forbidden colors (Japan)|en|Forbidden colors (Japan)|WD=Q11591157|lt=Forbidden color}}.
During the early Heian period, the Crown Prince’s ceremonial attire transitioned to red robes (九章服), and by the mid-Heian period, its use in court diminished. However, in formal dress (朝服), Ōni persisted, with {{ill|oshidori|ja|鴛鴦丸|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/oshidori|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (mandarin duck) patterns becoming standardized.
By the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period}}, the practice of a11ointing Crown Princes ceased, leading to a temporary discontinuation of the Ōni robe. The custom revived in the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}} with the investiture of Crown Princes like Prince Asahito (later {{ill|Emperor Higashiyama|en|Emperor Higashiyama|WD=Q313801|lt=Emperor Higashiyama}}). However, records suggest some Crown Princes initially wore reddish-brown robes called "red robes," a term potentially synonymous with Ōni.
In the {{ill|Reiwa era|en|Reiwa era|WD=Q62609017|lt=Reiwa era}}, the robe is worn by the imperial heir, {{ill|Fumihito, Crown Prince of Japan|en|Fumihito, Crown Prince of Japan|WD=Q311174|lt=Fumihito, Prince Akishino}}, during ceremonies.<ref>[https://www.sankei.com/article/20201108-GFKCPJRR3RJDHLW3MWBDGY3BAY/ "Ceremonial attire 'rising sun's color' specified in the Nara period"], ''Sankei News'', November 8, 2020.</ref>
== Design ==
Since the time of Prince Asahito, the robe’s crest has featured a flower-shaped frame with a downward-facing {{ill|oshidori|ja|鴛鴦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/oshidori|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to オシドリ|1=Mandarin Duck|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q200339}} (mandarin duck) motif. This design remains unchanged.
According to regulations from the late Edo period, under both the {{ill|Yamashina family|ja|山科家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashina family|qq=}} and {{ill|Takakura family|ja|高倉家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takakura family|qq=}}, winter robes for mature Crown Princes used yellow silk linings, while younger princes wore matching yellow linings for their {{ill|Kettetsu|ja|闕腋袍|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kettetsu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 袍|1=hō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q134436881}}. The placement of crests on the fabric adhered to a fixed grid without staggered alignment, similar to the Emperor’s robes.
The material and weaving methods evolved in modern times, but the arrangement of patterns became less dense for younger princes. A controversy arose during the investiture of Crown Prince Hirohito (later {{ill|Hirohito|en|Hirohito|WD=Q34479|lt=Emperor Shōwa}}), questioning whether the single mandarin duck should be replaced by a paired design or even by a crane motif. Ultimately, the original pattern was retained based on consultations with Kyoto noble families.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Kikujin robe|ja|麹塵袍|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kikujin robe|qq=}}
== References ==
{{Reflist}}
== External Links == * [http://yuusoku.jp/ Yūsoku Culture Research Institute] * [http://www.iz2.or.jp/ Costume Museum]

However, in formal dress (朝服), Ōni persisted, with {{ill|oshidori|ja|鴛鴦丸|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/oshidori|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (mandarin duck) patterns becoming standardized.

However, records suggest some Crown Princes initially wore reddish-brown robes called "red robes," a term potentially synonymous with Ōni.
In the {{ill|Reiwa era|en|Reiwa era|WD=Q62609017|lt=Reiwa era}}, the robe is worn by the imperial heir, {{ill|Fumihito, Crown Prince of Japan|en|Fumihito, Crown Prince of Japan|WD=Q311174|lt=Fumihito, Prince Akishino}}, during ceremonies.<ref>[https://www.sankei.com/article/20201108-GFKCPJRR3RJDHLW3MWBDGY3BAY/ "Ceremonial attire 'rising sun's color' specified in the Nara period"], ''Sankei News'', November 8, 2020.</ref>

The custom revived in the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}} with the investiture of Crown Princes like Prince Asahito (later {{ill|Emperor Higashiyama|en|Emperor Higashiyama|WD=Q313801|lt=Emperor Higashiyama}}). However, records suggest some Crown Princes initially wore reddish-brown robes called "red robes," a term potentially synonymous with Ōni.
In the {{ill|Reiwa era|en|Reiwa era|WD=Q62609017|lt=Reiwa era}}, the robe is worn by the imperial heir, {{ill|Fumihito, Crown Prince of Japan|en|Fumihito, Crown Prince of Japan|WD=Q311174|lt=Fumihito, Prince Akishino}}, during ceremonies.<ref>[https://www.sankei.com/article/20201108-GFKCPJRR3RJDHLW3MWBDGY3BAY/ "Ceremonial attire 'rising sun's color' specified in the Nara period"], ''Sankei News'', November 8, 2020.</ref>
== Design ==
Since the time of Prince Asahito, the robe’s crest has featured a flower-shaped frame with a downward-facing {{ill|oshidori|ja|鴛鴦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/oshidori|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to オシドリ|1=Mandarin Duck|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q200339}} (mandarin duck) motif. This design remains unchanged.
According to regulations from the late Edo period, under both the {{ill|Yamashina family|ja|山科家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashina family|qq=}} and {{ill|Takakura family|ja|高倉家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takakura family|qq=}}, winter robes for mature Crown Princes used yellow silk linings, while younger princes wore matching yellow linings for their {{ill|Kettetsu|ja|闕腋袍|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kettetsu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 袍|1=hō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q134436881}}. The placement of crests on the fabric adhered to a fixed grid without staggered alignment, similar to the Emperor’s robes.
The material and weaving methods evolved in modern times, but the arrangement of patterns became less dense for younger princes. A controversy arose during the investiture of Crown Prince Hirohito (later {{ill|Hirohito|en|Hirohito|WD=Q34479|lt=Emperor Shōwa}}), questioning whether the single mandarin duck should be replaced by a paired design or even by a crane motif. Ultimately, the original pattern was retained based on consultations with Kyoto noble families.

{{Infobox webcolor|title=Ōni (Crown Prince's Ceremonial Color)|hex=F47A55}} [[File:Prince Naruhito wearing Sokutai.jpg|thumb|200px|On [[November 12, 1990]], Crown Prince {{ill|Naruhito|en|Naruhito|WD=Q217096|lt=Naruhito}} wearing the Ōni robe at the {{ill|2019 Japanese imperial transition|en|2019 Japanese imperial transition|WD=Q44023338|lt=Sokutai ceremony}} for Emperor {{ill|Akihito|en|Akihito|WD=Q37979|lt=Akihito}}.]] [[File:Emperor Naruhito and Empress Masako in formal wedding robes.jpg|thumb|300px|In {{ill|1993|en|1993|WD=Q2065|lt=1993}}, the wedding ceremony of Crown Prince Naruhito and [[:en:Masako Owada|Masako Owada]]]] '''Ōni robe''' (黄丹袍, おうにのほう) is the {{ill|Sokutai|en|Sokutai|WD=Q1156828|lt=sokutai}} attire worn by the {{ill|Crown prince|en|Crown prince|WD=Q207293|lt=Crown Prince of Japan}} or {{ill|imperial heir|ja|皇嗣|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/imperial heir|qq=}} during ceremonial occasions. The name is derived from the color {{ill|Ōni|ja|黄丹|1=Ōdan|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōni|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōdan|qq=}}, a shade achieved through a dyeing process using {{ill|Gardenia jasminoides|en|Gardenia jasminoides|WD=Q1072101|lt=Gardenia jasminoides}} and {{ill|safflower|ja|紅花|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/safflower|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ベニバナ|1=Carthamus tinctorius|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q156625}}.
== History ==
The {{ill|Kōrozen|ja|黄櫨染御袍|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōrozen|qq=}}, worn by the Emperor, originated in early {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}} as an adaptation of the Chinese ceremonial garment {{ill|Sha Huang|zh|赭黄袍|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sha Huang|qq=}}, replacing earlier white robes. In contrast, the origins of Ōni in Japan date back to the {{ill|Yōrō Code|en|Yōrō Code|WD=Q2575743|lt=Yōrō Code}} during the {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|WD=Q189178|lt=Nara period}}, where it was designated in the "Dress Code" for the Crown Prince’s ceremonial and formal robes. It is believed to continue the tradition of the {{ill|Hanezuiro|ja|朱華色|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hanezuiro|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 唐棣色}} lineage, unrelated to Chinese customs where early Tang princes wore purple robes.
Ōni symbolizes the rising sun, and its use was strictly reserved for the Crown Prince, designated as a {{ill|Forbidden colors (Japan)|en|Forbidden colors (Japan)|WD=Q11591157|lt=Forbidden color}}.
During the early Heian period, the Crown Prince’s ceremonial attire transitioned to red robes (九章服), and by the mid-Heian period, its use in court diminished. However, in formal dress (朝服), Ōni persisted, with {{ill|oshidori|ja|鴛鴦丸|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/oshidori|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (mandarin duck) patterns becoming standardized.
By the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period}}, the practice of a11ointing Crown Princes ceased, leading to a temporary discontinuation of the Ōni robe. The custom revived in the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}} with the investiture of Crown Princes like Prince Asahito (later {{ill|Emperor Higashiyama|en|Emperor Higashiyama|WD=Q313801|lt=Emperor Higashiyama}}). However, records suggest some Crown Princes initially wore reddish-brown robes called "red robes," a term potentially synonymous with Ōni.
In the {{ill|Reiwa era|en|Reiwa era|WD=Q62609017|lt=Reiwa era}}, the robe is worn by the imperial heir, {{ill|Fumihito, Crown Prince of Japan|en|Fumihito, Crown Prince of Japan|WD=Q311174|lt=Fumihito, Prince Akishino}}, during ceremonies.<ref>[https://www.sankei.com/article/20201108-GFKCPJRR3RJDHLW3MWBDGY3BAY/ "Ceremonial attire 'rising sun's color' specified in the Nara period"], ''Sankei News'', November 8, 2020.</ref>
== Design ==
Since the time of Prince Asahito, the robe’s crest has featured a flower-shaped frame with a downward-facing {{ill|oshidori|ja|鴛鴦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/oshidori|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to オシドリ|1=Mandarin Duck|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q200339}} (mandarin duck) motif. This design remains unchanged.
According to regulations from the late Edo period, under both the {{ill|Yamashina family|ja|山科家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashina family|qq=}} and {{ill|Takakura family|ja|高倉家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takakura family|qq=}}, winter robes for mature Crown Princes used yellow silk linings, while younger princes wore matching yellow linings for their {{ill|Kettetsu|ja|闕腋袍|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kettetsu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 袍|1=hō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q134436881}}. The placement of crests on the fabric adhered to a fixed grid without staggered alignment, similar to the Emperor’s robes.
The material and weaving methods evolved in modern times, but the arrangement of patterns became less dense for younger princes. A controversy arose during the investiture of Crown Prince Hirohito (later {{ill|Hirohito|en|Hirohito|WD=Q34479|lt=Emperor Shōwa}}), questioning whether the single mandarin duck should be replaced by a paired design or even by a crane motif. Ultimately, the original pattern was retained based on consultations with Kyoto noble families.

{{Infobox webcolor|title=Ōni (Crown Prince's Ceremonial Color)|hex=F47A55}} [[File:Prince Naruhito wearing Sokutai.jpg|thumb|200px|On [[November 12, 1990]], Crown Prince {{ill|Naruhito|en|Naruhito|WD=Q217096|lt=Naruhito|1=Naruhito}} wearing the Ōni robe at the {{ill|2019 Japanese imperial transition|en|2019 Japanese imperial transition|WD=Q44023338|lt=Sokutai ceremony|1=2019 Japanese imperial transition}} for Emperor {{ill|Akihito|en|Akihito|WD=Q37979|lt=Akihito|1=Akihito}}.]] [[File:Emperor Naruhito and Empress Masako in formal wedding robes.jpg|thumb|300px|In {{ill|1993|en|1993|WD=Q2065|lt=1993|1=1993}}, the wedding ceremony of Crown Prince Naruhito and [[:en:Masako Owada|Masako Owada]]]] '''Ōni robe''' (黄丹袍, おうにのほう) is the {{ill|Sokutai|en|Sokutai|WD=Q1156828|lt=sokutai|1=Sokutai}} attire worn by the {{ill|Crown prince|en|Crown prince|WD=Q207293|lt=Crown Prince of Japan|1=Crown prince}} or {{ill|imperial heir|ja|皇嗣|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/imperial heir|qq=}} during ceremonial occasions. The name is derived from the color {{ill|Ōni|ja|黄丹|1=Ōdan|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōni|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōdan|qq=}}, a shade achieved through a dyeing process using {{ill|Gardenia jasminoides|en|Gardenia jasminoides|WD=Q1072101|lt=Gardenia jasminoides|1=Gardenia jasminoides}} and {{ill|safflower|ja|紅花|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/safflower|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ベニバナ|1=Safflower|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q156625}}.
== History ==
The {{ill|Kōrozen|ja|黄櫨染御袍|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōrozen|qq=}}, worn by the Emperor, originated in early {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}} as an adaptation of the Chinese ceremonial garment {{ill|Sha Huang|zh|赭黄袍|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sha Huang|qq=}}, replacing earlier white robes. In contrast, the origins of Ōni in Japan date back to the {{ill|Yōrō Code|en|Yōrō Code|WD=Q2575743|lt=Yōrō Code|1=Yōrō Code}} during the {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|WD=Q189178|lt=Nara period|1=Nara period}}, where it was designated in the "Dress Code" for the Crown Prince’s ceremonial and formal robes. It is believed to continue the tradition of the {{ill|Hanezuiro|ja|朱華色|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hanezuiro|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 唐棣色}} lineage, unrelated to Chinese customs where early Tang princes wore purple robes.
Ōni symbolizes the rising sun, and its use was strictly reserved for the Crown Prince, designated as a {{ill|Forbidden colors (Japan)|en|Forbidden colors (Japan)|WD=Q11591157|lt=Forbidden color|1=Forbidden colors (Japan)}}.
During the early Heian period, the Crown Prince’s ceremonial attire transitioned to red robes (九章服), and by the mid-Heian period, its use in court diminished. However, in formal dress (朝服), Ōni persisted, with {{ill|oshidori|ja|鴛鴦丸|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/oshidori|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (mandarin duck) patterns becoming standardized.
By the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period|1=Muromachi period}}, the practice of a11ointing Crown Princes ceased, leading to a temporary discontinuation of the Ōni robe. The custom revived in the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}} with the investiture of Crown Princes like Prince Asahito (later {{ill|Emperor Higashiyama|en|Emperor Higashiyama|WD=Q313801|lt=Emperor Higashiyama|1=Emperor Higashiyama}}). However, records suggest some Crown Princes initially wore reddish-brown robes called "red robes," a term potentially synonymous with Ōni.
In the {{ill|Reiwa era|en|Reiwa era|WD=Q62609017|lt=Reiwa era|1=Reiwa era}}, the robe is worn by the imperial heir, {{ill|Fumihito, Crown Prince of Japan|en|Fumihito, Crown Prince of Japan|WD=Q311174|lt=Fumihito, Prince Akishino|1=Fumihito, Crown Prince of Japan}}, during ceremonies.<ref>[https://www.sankei.com/article/20201108-GFKCPJRR3RJDHLW3MWBDGY3BAY/ "Ceremonial attire 'rising sun's color' specified in the Nara period"], ''Sankei News'', November 8, 2020.</ref>
== Design ==
Since the time of Prince Asahito, the robe’s crest has featured a flower-shaped frame with a downward-facing {{ill|oshidori|ja|鴛鴦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/oshidori|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to オシドリ|1=Mandarin duck|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q200339}} (mandarin duck) motif. This design remains unchanged.
According to regulations from the late Edo period, under both the {{ill|Yamashina family|ja|山科家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashina family|qq=}} and {{ill|Takakura family|ja|高倉家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takakura family|qq=}}, winter robes for mature Crown Princes used yellow silk linings, while younger princes wore matching yellow linings for their {{ill|Kettetsu|ja|闕腋袍|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kettetsu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 袍|1=hō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q134436881}}. The placement of crests on the fabric adhered to a fixed grid without staggered alignment, similar to the Emperor’s robes.
The material and weaving methods evolved in modern times, but the arrangement of patterns became less dense for younger princes. A controversy arose during the investiture of Crown Prince Hirohito (later {{ill|Hirohito|en|Hirohito|WD=Q34479|lt=Emperor Shōwa|1=Hirohito}}), questioning whether the single mandarin duck should be replaced by a paired design or even by a crane motif. Ultimately, the original pattern was retained based on consultations with Kyoto noble families.

The custom revived in the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}} with the investiture of Crown Princes like Prince Asahito (later {{ill|Emperor Higashiyama|en|Emperor Higashiyama|WD=Q313801|lt=Emperor Higashiyama|1=Emperor Higashiyama}}). However, records suggest some Crown Princes initially wore reddish-brown robes called "red robes," a term potentially synonymous with Ōni.
In the {{ill|Reiwa era|en|Reiwa era|WD=Q62609017|lt=Reiwa era|1=Reiwa era}}, the robe is worn by the imperial heir, {{ill|Fumihito, Crown Prince of Japan|en|Fumihito, Crown Prince of Japan|WD=Q311174|lt=Fumihito, Prince Akishino|1=Fumihito, Crown Prince of Japan}}, during ceremonies.<ref>[https://www.sankei.com/article/20201108-GFKCPJRR3RJDHLW3MWBDGY3BAY/ "Ceremonial attire 'rising sun's color' specified in the Nara period"], ''Sankei News'', November 8, 2020.</ref>
== Design ==
Since the time of Prince Asahito, the robe’s crest has featured a flower-shaped frame with a downward-facing {{ill|oshidori|ja|鴛鴦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/oshidori|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to オシドリ|1=Mandarin duck|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q200339}} (mandarin duck) motif. This design remains unchanged.
According to regulations from the late Edo period, under both the {{ill|Yamashina family|ja|山科家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashina family|qq=}} and {{ill|Takakura family|ja|高倉家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takakura family|qq=}}, winter robes for mature Crown Princes used yellow silk linings, while younger princes wore matching yellow linings for their {{ill|Kettetsu|ja|闕腋袍|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kettetsu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 袍|1=hō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q134436881}}. The placement of crests on the fabric adhered to a fixed grid without staggered alignment, similar to the Emperor’s robes.
The material and weaving methods evolved in modern times, but the arrangement of patterns became less dense for younger princes. A controversy arose during the investiture of Crown Prince Hirohito (later {{ill|Hirohito|en|Hirohito|WD=Q34479|lt=Emperor Shōwa|1=Hirohito}}), questioning whether the single mandarin duck should be replaced by a paired design or even by a crane motif. Ultimately, the original pattern was retained based on consultations with Kyoto noble families.

{{Infobox webcolor|title=Ōni (Crown Prince's Ceremonial Color)|hex=F47A55}} [[File:Prince Naruhito wearing Sokutai.jpg|thumb|200px|On [[November 12, 1990]], Crown Prince {{ill|Naruhito|en|Naruhito|WD=Q217096|lt=Naruhito|1=Naruhito}} wearing the Ōni robe at the {{ill|2019 Japanese imperial transition|en|2019 Japanese imperial transition|WD=Q44023338|lt=Sokutai ceremony|1=2019 Japanese imperial transition}} for Emperor {{ill|Akihito|en|Akihito|WD=Q37979|lt=Akihito|1=Akihito}}.]] [[File:Emperor Naruhito and Empress Masako in formal wedding robes.jpg|thumb|300px|In {{ill|1993|en|1993|WD=Q2065|lt=1993|1=1993}}, the wedding ceremony of Crown Prince Naruhito and [[:en:Masako Owada|Masako Owada]]]] '''Ōni robe''' (黄丹袍, おうにのほう) is the {{ill|Sokutai|en|Sokutai|WD=Q1156828|lt=sokutai|1=Sokutai}} attire worn by the {{ill|Crown prince|en|Crown prince|WD=Q207293|lt=Crown Prince of Japan|1=Crown prince}} or {{ill|imperial heir|ja|皇嗣|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/imperial heir|qq=}} during ceremonial occasions. The name is derived from the color {{ill|Ōni|ja|黄丹|1=Ōdan|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōni|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōdan|qq=}}, a shade achieved through a dyeing process using {{ill|Gardenia jasminoides|en|Gardenia jasminoides|WD=Q1072101|lt=Gardenia jasminoides|1=Gardenia jasminoides}} and {{ill|safflower|ja|紅花|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/safflower|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ベニバナ|1=Safflower|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q156625}}.
== History ==
The {{ill|Kōrozen|ja|黄櫨染御袍|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōrozen|qq=}}, worn by the Emperor, originated in early {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}} as an adaptation of the Chinese ceremonial garment {{ill|Sha Huang|zh|赭黄袍|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sha Huang|qq=}}, replacing earlier white robes. In contrast, the origins of Ōni in Japan date back to the {{ill|Yōrō Code|en|Yōrō Code|WD=Q2575743|lt=Yōrō Code|1=Yōrō Code}} during the {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|WD=Q189178|lt=Nara period|1=Nara period}}, where it was designated in the "Dress Code" for the Crown Prince’s ceremonial and formal robes. It is believed to continue the tradition of the {{ill|Hanezuiro|ja|朱華色|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hanezuiro|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 唐棣色}} lineage, unrelated to Chinese customs where early Tang princes wore purple robes.
Ōni symbolizes the rising sun, and its use was strictly reserved for the Crown Prince, designated as a {{ill|Forbidden colors (Japan)|en|Forbidden colors (Japan)|WD=Q11591157|lt=Forbidden color|1=Forbidden colors (Japan)}}.
During the early Heian period, the Crown Prince’s ceremonial attire transitioned to red robes (九章服), and by the mid-Heian period, its use in court diminished. However, in formal dress (朝服), Ōni persisted, with {{ill|oshidori|ja|鴛鴦丸|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/oshidori|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (mandarin duck) patterns becoming standardized.
By the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period|1=Muromachi period}}, the practice of a11ointing Crown Princes ceased, leading to a temporary discontinuation of the Ōni robe. The custom revived in the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}} with the investiture of Crown Princes like Prince Asahito (later {{ill|Emperor Higashiyama|en|Emperor Higashiyama|WD=Q313801|lt=Emperor Higashiyama|1=Emperor Higashiyama}}). However, records suggest some Crown Princes initially wore reddish-brown robes called "red robes," a term potentially synonymous with Ōni.
In the {{ill|Reiwa era|en|Reiwa era|WD=Q62609017|lt=Reiwa era|1=Reiwa era}}, the robe is worn by the imperial heir, {{ill|Fumihito, Crown Prince of Japan|en|Fumihito, Crown Prince of Japan|WD=Q311174|lt=Fumihito, Prince Akishino|1=Fumihito, Crown Prince of Japan}}, during ceremonies.<ref>[https://www.sankei.com/article/20201108-GFKCPJRR3RJDHLW3MWBDGY3BAY/ "Ceremonial attire 'rising sun's color' specified in the Nara period"], ''Sankei News'', November 8, 2020.</ref>
== Design ==
Since the time of Prince Asahito, the robe’s crest has featured a flower-shaped frame with a downward-facing {{ill|oshidori|ja|鴛鴦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/oshidori|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to オシドリ|1=Mandarin duck|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q200339}} (mandarin duck) motif. This design remains unchanged.
According to regulations from the late Edo period, under both the {{ill|Yamashina family|ja|山科家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashina family|qq=}} and {{ill|Takakura family|ja|高倉家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takakura family|qq=}}, winter robes for mature Crown Princes used yellow silk linings, while younger princes wore matching yellow linings for their {{ill|Kettetsu|ja|闕腋袍|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kettetsu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 袍|1=hō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q134436881}}. The placement of crests on the fabric adhered to a fixed grid without staggered alignment, similar to the Emperor’s robes.
The material and weaving methods evolved in modern times, but the arrangement of patterns became less dense for younger princes. A controversy arose during the investiture of Crown Prince Hirohito (later {{ill|Hirohito|en|Hirohito|WD=Q34479|lt=Emperor Shōwa|1=Hirohito}}), questioning whether the single mandarin duck should be replaced by a paired design or even by a crane motif. Ultimately, the original pattern was retained based on consultations with Kyoto noble families.
